chapters.summary: Council staff welcomes attendees to day three of FY27 executive budget hearings, covering the Committee on Finance joined with Committees on Children and Youth and Immigration. Staff reminds attendees to silence devices and not approach the dais.
chapters.title: Staff opens FY27 Executive Budget Hearing for Finance, Children & Youth, and Immigration Committees

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good morning and welcome to today's New York City Council Executive Budget Hearing for the Committee on Finance, joined with the Committee on Children and Youth, and joined with the Committee on Immigration.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: At this time, please silence all electronic devices and no one may approach the dais at any time.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Chairs, we are ready to begin.

chapters.summary: Councilmember Linda Lee gavels in and introduces the hearing on ACS's FY27 executive budget of $3.51 billion, noting a $89.6M increase from preliminary plan. She highlights child care vouchers, Promise NYC baselining, and the 25,000-child voucher waitlist.
chapters.title: Chair Linda Lee opens ACS hearing with FY27 budget overview

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, wait, oh, got a gavel.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Always forget that part.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Good morning and welcome to day three of the fiscal FY27 executive budget hearings.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm Councilmember Linda Lee and today's hearings will begin with the Administration for Children's Services followed by Department of Youth and Community Development and the Mayor's Office of Immigrant Affairs.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm pleased to be joined by my colleague and dear friend, Councilmember Althea Stevens, Chair of the Committee on Children and Youth.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And today we have been joined by Councilmember Marte, Councilmember Shulman, Councilmember Epstein, Councilmember Joseph, Councilmember Wong, and Deputy Speaker Williams is on Zoom.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Welcome, Commissioner Rebecca Jones-Gaston.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Week two, or day seven, rather, day six, to you and your team, and of course your amazing team.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you all for joining us today to answer our questions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: On May 12, 2026, the administration released the executive financial plan for fiscal 2026 to 2027.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: 30, with a proposed FY27 budget of $124.7 billion.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The Administration for Children's Services proposed fiscal FY27 budget of $3.51 billion represents

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: 2.7% of the administration's proposed FY27 budget in the executive plan, and this is an increase of $89.6 million, or 2.6%,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: from the $3.42 billion originally budgeted in the FY27 preliminary plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: This increase results from several actions, including additional funding added for child care vouchers, foster care, staffing across the agency, and a baselining of the Promise NYC program.

chapters.summary: Lee notes ACS headcount is 562 below FY26 budgeted levels. She calls on the Mayor to apply for the state Raise the Age hardship waiver for reimbursement going back to FY19, and urges progress on reducing the voucher waitlist. She previews questions on savings methodology and $200M in state at-risk youth funding.
chapters.title: Chair Lee raises Raise the Age waiver and at-risk youth funding

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: As of April 2026, ACS's headcount was 562 less than their FY26 budgeted headcount.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In the Council's preliminary budget response, we called on the Mayor to apply for the state waiver that would allow the City to apply for reimbursement of its expenditures across fiscal 2019

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: to 2026, related to the implementation of the State's Raise the Age Act.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Further in our response, we called on the administration to continue to invest in child care vouchers by baselining the Promise NYC program.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In our preliminary budget hearing, we encouraged the administration to explore ways to end the waiting list practice for child care vouchers, which now stands at approximately 25,000 children.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: While we were pleased to see the Promise New York City baselined and some additional funding added for child care vouchers, I hope to see

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: progress made in reducing the voucher wait list and ensuring these families are able to receive the vital child care services that they need, whether through ACS or other agencies.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I look forward to hearing from our new commissioner on how ACS plans to address the urgent challenge of providing child care and continue to equitably serve families amid citywide savings requirements.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: My questions today will largely focus on areas in which ACS was able to realize savings, the agency's process and methodology in identifying these savings, and updates on the nearly $200 million in state funding designated for at-risk youth.

chapters.summary: Chair Stevens discusses new needs of $55.7M added in FY26 executive plan, growing to $104.6M in FY27. She emphasizes the city must not be balanced on the backs of young people, and raises concerns about savings methodology, state funding plans, and the hardship application for Raise the Age.
chapters.title: Chair Althea Stevens delivers opening statement on ACS savings impact on youth

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I now want to turn it over to my co-chair for this hearing, Councilmember Stevens, for her opening statement.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Good morning and thank you, Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: My name is Althea Stevens and I'm serves as the chair of Committee on Children and Youth.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: For this first part of our hearing this morning, I look forward to discussing changes to ACS budgets since the preliminary plan.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: The executive budget included investments in several areas, including child care vouchers, detention facilities, and staffing across the agency.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: New needs of $55.7 million were added to the fiscal 2026 in the executive plan, growing to $104.6 million in fiscal 2027 and dropping to $80.7 million in fiscal 2028 and in the outer years.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Additionally, other adjustments in fiscal 2026 totaled $547.6 million, dropping to $2 million in fiscal 2027 and in the outer years.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: While the city's budget now has been balanced without property taxes increased, all the agencies were required to meet savings and vacancy reduction benchmarks.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: As agencies providing frontline services to some of the city's most vulnerable residents, understanding the impact of these savings targeted ACS is critical.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: My theme for today, and will always continue to be, the city will not be considered affordable if it's not affordable for young people.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Because if we don't invest in our young people on the front end, we then ultimately invest in them on the back end when they are living and doing things that are not things that we want to see.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I look forward to discussing how ACS responded to the mayor's savings requirements, their methodology, the rationale, and identifying areas where savings could be made.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: could be realized, and most critically, the effects on services or programming that these savings might have.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I'm also interested in understanding how the agency is working with the state partners to maximize the funding in vital services such as child care vouchers, preventative services, and programming.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I'm also really curious to hear about the plan for the money that was allocated from the governor because in our

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: preliminary hearing, that plan still had not been discussed with us and want to make sure that the money that was allocated for youth services and the raise the age that we are thinking about and also would like to hear if we're going to

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: put in a hardship application.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: In the Council's responsibility to ensure that the city budget is fair, transparent, and accountable to New Yorkers, as the Chair of Children and Youth Committee, I will continue to push for accountability and transparency in the ACS budget.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Amidst the needs to eliminate budget gaps and meet saving requirements, our responsibility is in acting a budget that reflects the needs and interests of our city's youth, taking on even higher importance.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Like the Mayor has often repeated, this budget must not be balanced on the back of working people or young people.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I'm adding the or because nobody keeps talking about the young people.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: So today, through and throughout the examination of AC budget, we ensure that this is the case.

chapters.summary: Lee and Stevens thank their respective staff and the Council Finance Division. They remind attendees that public testimony will be held Wednesday, June 10th at 9:30 AM, and that nonprofit partners are welcome to attend and listen.
chapters.title: Chairs Lee and Stevens thank staff and announce public testimony date

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Before we get started, I would like to take a quick moment to thank both my staff and the entire council fiscal division for their efforts in preparing for this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Margaret, Julia,

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Richard, Ria, Elizabeth, and my Deputy Chief of Staff, Greer, and the entire A-Team back in the Suite 16.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: At this time, I'll pass it back to Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Before we get started, just wanted to take a moment to thank the entire Council Finance Team and Division for preparing today's hearing, especially Julia Haramis and Margaret Barnsley

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: for today's hearing, and of course, Brian Sarfo and all the finance analysts and support staff who make it all work.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And just as a reminder, for this year's executive budget hearings, because we're on a tighter timeline than we normally are, we're going to be doing all of the public testimony on Wednesday, June 10th, beginning at 9.30.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I see some of our partners here from our nonprofits that are here with us, which I'm glad, actually, because I think it's always important to be in the room and listen to the feedback.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then also, hopefully, it'll help inform the questions you do have when we hopefully will see you back on the 10th, June 10th, Wednesday, starting at 9.30 a.m.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I will turn it now over to our committee counsel to swear in the witnesses for their testimony.

chapters.summary: Committee counsel swears in Commissioner Rebecca Jones-Gaston and six Deputy Commissioners for their testimony before the committees.
chapters.title: Committee counsel swears in ACS Commissioner and Deputy Commissioners

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good morning, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before this committee and to respond honestly to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Commissioner Jones-Gaston?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner Pletnikoff?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner Gendel?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner Mendez?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner Linares?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner Welcomer?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: And Deputy Commissioner Ginsberg?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: New ACS Commissioner Rebecca Jones-Gaston shares her personal background in child welfare, her career from frontline practice to federal leadership under the Biden-Harris administration, and her commitment to equity, prevention, and partnership.
chapters.title: Commissioner Jones-Gaston introduces herself and her personal connection to child welfare

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: My name is Rebecca Jones-Gaston, and I am honored to serve as the Commissioner for the Administration of Children's Services.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I'd like to thank Chair Stevens, Chair Lee, and the members of the Children and Youth and Finance Committees for convening today's hearing on the Executive Budget for City Fiscal Year 2027.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I am joined by my colleagues, Deputy Commissioner for Financial Services, Margaret Plentikoff, Deputy Commissioner for External Affairs, Stephanie Glendell,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner for the Division of Youth and Family Justice, Nancy Ginsberg, Deputy Commissioner for the Family Services Division, Louisa Linares,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner for Family Permanency Services, Ina Mendez, and Deputy Commissioner for the Division of Child and Family Well-Being, Elizabeth Volcomore.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Before I begin, I want to acknowledge Melissa Hester for her steady and compassionate leadership as interim commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: During a period of transition, Melissa guided this agency with care, integrity, and deep commitment to children and families.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And I want to thank her and congratulate her on her new role as Executive Deputy Commissioner of People, Operations, and Technology.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I often say that I was born into the

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Child welfare is not simply the work I do, it is personal to me.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: It has shaped my life, my sense of purpose, and my understanding of what children, youth, and families need not only to survive, but to heal, grow, and thrive.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Because I know personally what systems can mean in the life of a child, I approach this role with both urgency and hope.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: My professional journey has taken me from the frontline practice in child protection and foster care to leadership roles at the state and federal levels, including serving as commissioner

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: of the Administration on Children, Youth, and Families during the Biden-Harris administration.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Across every role,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Every system and every community, one lesson has remained constant.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Meaningful and lasting change only happens when we remain grounded in the voices, experiences, strengths, and hopes of the people most impacted by our decisions.

chapters.summary: Jones-Gaston discusses her belief that safety and connection must coexist, that children are not cases and families are not problems, and her commitment to investing in a skilled, supported workforce. She outlines her approach to juvenile justice transformation.
chapters.title: Commissioner outlines ACS philosophy on safety, prevention, and workforce investment

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: That belief will guide my leadership at ACS.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I also recognize that this work does not happen in isolation.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The well-being of children and families depends on strong partnership across systems and sectors, including providers, advocates, community leaders, and our partners in the judiciary.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Lasting progress requires all of us working together with shared accountability and shared commitment to safety, healing, fairness, and opportunity.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I believe deeply.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: that safety and connection must coexist.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Children should be safe, and families should have the support they need to remain together whenever possible.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Communities should be seen not simply through the lens of need, but through the lens of strength, wisdom, and possibility.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Children are not cases.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Families are not problems to solve.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Communities are not deficits to manage.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: They are people with stories, strengths, hopes, and potential.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And our responsibility is to shape systems that recognize humanity and respond with accountability, compassion, and intention.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: As Commissioner, I'm committed to continuing the work of strengthening child welfare system that is more responsive, more equitable, more preventive, and more humane.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: That means ensuring child protective intention is focused on where children are truly unsafe.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: While continuing to invest in the community-based and family-centered supports that help families thrive before crisis occurs.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: It means listening closely to young people and families with lived experiences and recognizing them as partners in shaping the solutions.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And it means continuing to transform how we support young people involved in the juvenile justice system, seeing them not only for the worst moment in their lives, but for the worst moment in their lives.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: In their lives, but for the worst moment in their lives, but for the worst moment in their lives.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: their potential, resilience, and future.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I also want to be clear about the tone and the spirit I hope to bring to this role.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I believe in stewardship, I believe in partnership, I believe in accountability rooted in compassion, and I believe in systems change

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: happens when we are willing to listen, to learn, and to act with informed intentionality.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I also believe none of this work is possible without a skilled, supported, and well-resourced workforce.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Every day, ACF staff provide our partners, attorneys,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Clinicians, case planners, direct care staff, and so many others carry enormous responsibility on behalf of children and families, often under incredibly complex circumstances.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: If we want stronger outcomes for children and for families, we must continue investing in the people who make these outcomes possible.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I look forward to building an honest and constructive partnership with this Council as we work together on behalf of New York City's children and families.

chapters.summary: Jones-Gaston presents the $3.51B FY27 budget including $1.4B in city funds. She thanks Chief Savings Officer Margaret Pletnikoff and highlights the Mamdani administration's prioritization of expanding quality child care throughout the city.
chapters.title: Commissioner presents FY27 executive budget overview and savings approach

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Today, I'll provide an overview of the FY 2027 Executive Budget, which is projected to be $3.51 billion, including $1.4 billion in city funds.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: As you know, the Mondani administration asked each agency to identify a chief savings officer to carefully examine opportunities.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: for savings and efficiencies.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I want to thank ACS's Chief Savings Officer, Margaret Plentikoff, for leading this work thoughtfully, with continued focus on minimizing impacts on children, families, and providers who depend on our services.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Affordable child care is essential for families.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So I am extremely pleased that the Momdani administration has prioritized expanding across the quality child care throughout the city.

chapters.summary: Jones-Gaston explains state-city budgetary agreements enabling recertification of low-income child care vouchers and support for HRA-served families. She announces Promise NYC is baselined at $25M, supporting approximately 1,000 slots, creating certainty and stability.
chapters.title: Commissioner details child care voucher agreements and Promise NYC baselining

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: ACS's budget furthers the commitment in a number of ways.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Due to the advocacy from the Mamdani administration, the City Council, and many others, the state and city have come to a number of budgetary agreements that will enable New York City to expand and maintain access to early childhood education.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: As it relates to ACS,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The funding from the city and the state in ACS's budget will enable us to continue to recertify the vast majority of families receiving low-income child care vouchers from ACS.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The funding will also allow the city to support the growing need for child care vouchers among families on cash assistance who are served by the Human Resources Administration.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: In addition, we are extremely pleased to share that the FY27 executive budget restores and baselines Promise NYC at $25 million.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: This funding will enable ACS to maintain city-funded, community-based child care.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: This funding

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: supports approximately 1,000 slots.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Historically, Promise NYC was funded for one year at a time.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Baselining the funding for this crucial program creates certainty and stability.

chapters.summary: Jones-Gaston announces 11 community partnerships with expanded community ambassador roles. She details ACS's 44 provider agencies operating 127 prevention programs with 10,277 slots, and notes community referrals increased from 12% in January 2023 to 27% in March 2026.
chapters.title: Commissioner discusses community partnerships and prevention services

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I have dedicated my career to identifying ways to support and uplift families and communities so that they can be safe and thrive.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: As I take on this role, I remain committed to lifting up family-centered, prevention-driven solutions that improve outcomes and transform how systems support children, families, and communities.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I am committed to keeping children safe by providing ACS child protective staff with the tools, supervision, and training they need to make sound decisions.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And I am committed to ensuring ACS helps to provide families and communities with the support they need to succeed.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: ACS will soon announce the providers recommended for the 11 community partnerships across the city.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Community partnerships are neighborhood coalitions that enhance child and family well-being through strategies responsive to each community's specific wants and needs.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: By listening to the voices and ideas of community members, the community partnerships work to increase coordination among service providers, connect families to resources, and fill programming gaps.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The new contracts will expand the role of community ambassadors, trusted community residents who build relationships with and connect families

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: to information and resources.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I'm looking forward to the implementation of these new contracts and the positive impact the partnerships will continue to have.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: ACS currently contracts with 44 provider agencies operating 127 programs for family services, previously referred to as prevention services.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Across the system, ACS contracts for 10,277 slots, meaning more than 10,000 families can receive services at any one point in time.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: In calendar year 2025, a little over 30,000 children in 14,000 families received child welfare, family support, and therapeutic treatment services.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: ACS and our providers have been intentional about supporting communities, engaging families, and connecting them to resources.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Through this work, we've increased community referrals from 12% of all families served in January 2023 to 27% of them in March of 2026.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: These include referrals from schools, healthcare providers, and families themselves.

chapters.summary: Jones-Gaston explains the budget includes closing four prevention programs totaling 360 slots for $2.7M CTL savings, with assurances that no family will lose services and that ACS will monitor community needs.
chapters.title: Commissioner explains prevention contract closures for savings

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: As part of our efforts to look closely at our budget and possible efficiencies, this budget includes closing four programs totaling 360 slots for savings of $2.7 million

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: CTL and $7.1 million gross.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: We will be working closely with the four providers as well.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: as the providers in the same catchment area to ensure that no family loses services.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: We will also continue to monitor community needs and make sure families continue to have access to services.

chapters.summary: Jones-Gaston reports fewer than 6,300 children in foster care, down from 42,000 when ACS was created. She details the $40M Youth Safety and Success Initiative funding Fair Futures coaches, College Choice program expansion, and the new Career Choice program.
chapters.title: Commissioner highlights historic low foster care numbers and Youth Safety Initiative

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: In New York City, the number of children in foster care is at a historic low, with fewer than 6,300 children in care, down from 13,000 a decade ago and close to 42,000 when ACS was created 30 years ago.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Our work at ACS centers on continuing to reduce the number of children in care, both by preventing children from entering in the first place and from intensifying efforts to reunify children more quickly.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I am committed to working with the ACS team and our providers to continue this reduction.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: For those children and youth in our care, it is essential that we provide them with the resources and skills that they need to become successful and thriving New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: This budget includes important actions that will enable us to do this.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The executive budget includes a number of budgetary reductions in the foster care area that will have no impact on children, youth, families, or providers,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: as the savings are from underspending or surpluses in specific budget codes, which I'll explain in more detail.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: As you recall, last fiscal year, ACS's budget included the Youth Safety and Success Initiative, which was a $40 million investment in programs for youth in foster care.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: and the justice system.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Specifically, as it relates to foster care, the initiative funded an expansion of the number of youth who could be served by Fair Futures coaches and tutors,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: the number of youth participating in the College Choice program, and the creation of a new program, Career Choice.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: As a result of this investment, we see more youth getting connected to Fair Futures coaches, and there are over 470 youth enrolled in College Choice, up from 430 last year.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: In addition, we've created and launched Career Choice, a new program that builds off the success of College Choice

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: by connecting young people in foster care to similar financial, social, and academic support when they elect to attend a vocational program or a job readiness program instead of college.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The program was launched in October, and the first 10 youth are now enrolled in Career Choice programming.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: In addition, we are on track for 136 youth to participate in the Department of Youth and Community Development's Advance and Earn program this fiscal year, which is also a part of Career Choice.

chapters.summary: Jones-Gaston explains the executive budget aligns Fair Futures and Career Choice spending with programmatic ramp-up, saving $2M CTL in FY26-27 for Fair Futures and $405K CTL for Career Choice, with no impact on services. Full ramp-up to 5,400 youth by FY29.
chapters.title: Commissioner explains Fair Futures and Career Choice budget adjustments

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The executive budget better aligns anticipated spending levels of both Fair Futures and Career Choice with no impact on services to better match programmatic ramp up and allocated funding.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Specifically for Fair Futures, we plan for the program to grow in budget and use

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: served in both FY27 and 28, while saving about 2 million CTL in FY26 and 27 and 1.1 million in FY28.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: We still anticipate full ramp up to 42.7 million and 54 youth by FY29.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Similarly, there is a 405,000 CTL and 1.1 million gross reduction in career choice in the current fiscal year 2026 due to the savings from the initial startup time needed for the program.

chapters.summary: Jones-Gaston explains $1.4M CTL reduction in Preparing Youth for Adulthood services due to surplus, $233K CTL reduction for adoption recruitment contracts no longer maintained since 2015, and $97.5M CTL to address the Title IV-E budget gap from federal reimbursement ineligibility.
chapters.title: Commissioner details foster care budget reductions from surpluses

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: All residential and specialized family foster care providers receive $3,500 for each youth ages 14 to 21 in their care for independent living services, referred to as preparing youth for adulthood.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: These services help youth develop life skills through workshops and programs such as financial literacy, resume writing, college applications, grocery shopping, banking, and driver's education.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The executive budget includes an annual reduction of 1.4 million CTL, 3.75 million gross, due to a surplus in the budget code.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: There will be no impact on providers or youth.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Providers will continue to receive the $3,500 for each youth age 14 to 21.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The executive budget also includes 233,000 CTL, 1.1 million gross.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: reduction for adoption recruitment contracts, which ACS no longer maintains and has not since 2015.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Adoption and foster parent recruitment is now managed by our foster care providers and their current contracts include recruitment funding.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Thus, there is no interruption in service and we do not anticipate any impact on providers or families.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The executive budget includes $97.5 million in CTL funding to address the Title IV-E budget gap the ACS has been carrying due to the way the federal government reimburses for foster care.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Specifically, while federal Title IV-E funding for foster care is a federal entitlement, it is only an entitlement for those children and youth who are federally eligible.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: While there are a number of eligibility requirements, family income is the one that causes most children to be ineligible for federal reimbursement.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: To be eligible, current income of the child's family must be such that they would have been eligible for AFDC, which was the predecessor of TANF, in 1996.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: This has never been adjusted to inflation, nor the current public assistance program.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: There was a period of time where the gap was being filled by federal government funds through the 4E waiver, but that ended in 2018.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: This addition

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: This additional funding fills the baselines, the gap ACS has been carrying.

chapters.summary: Jones-Gaston highlights $1.6M for immigration legal services and fees for children in foster care, noting ACS's long history of working with pro bono providers on special immigrant juvenile status and new Trump administration fee increases.
chapters.title: Commissioner discusses immigration legal services for foster care youth

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The executive budget also includes $1.6 million for immigration legal services and fees for children and youth in foster care.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Since at least 2006, ACS has been working with legal service providers to assist youth in care by obtaining special immigrant juvenile status.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: ACS is extremely grateful to these providers who have helped countless youth in care get green cards all pro bono.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Given the increasing demands for immigration legal assistance, ACS will now be able to provide attorneys handling these matters with $2,000 per child.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: In addition,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The Trump administration recently created new fees and increased existing fees for various immigration relief applications, and this additional funding will provide reimbursement for those fees.

chapters.summary: Jones-Gaston reports 362 youth in secure detention, 66 in non-secure detention, and 128 in close-to-home. The budget includes $6.3M for Horizon exercise pavilion, maintenance, supplies, and conversion of a 6-bed to 9-bed non-secure placement facility, plus capital needs for HVAC and IT.
chapters.title: Commissioner details juvenile justice facility funding and capital needs

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: ACS continues to focus on meeting the needs of the increased number of youth across our juvenile justice continuum.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: As of May 13, 2026,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: There were 362 youth in secure detention, 66 youth in non-secure detention, and 128 youth in close to home.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The executive budget includes funding to support operations, maintenance, and capacity needs at our facilities.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The budget includes $6.3 million baselined for juvenile justice facility needs associated with the creation of the exercise pavilion at Horizon,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: including the additional funding needed for maintenance repairs and operations of the existing space.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: This funding will also be used for additional supplies needed for the population of youth in detention, such as uniforms, toiletries, bedding, and food services.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Finally, a portion of this initiative is additional funding needed to support the conversion of a six-bed non-secure placement facility to a nine-bed non-secure placement facility

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: to help with the increased close-to-home census.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The executive budget also includes additional capital needs for our juvenile justice programs, including funding for the Horizon Annex, HVACs at Horizon and

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Crossroads, as well as our other repairs and IT equipment at Horizon and Crossroads.

chapters.summary: Jones-Gaston concludes by emphasizing the budget reflects investment in children and families, acknowledges federal policy pressures, and commits to intentional transformation rooted in equity, community partnership, and accountability.
chapters.title: Commissioner closes testimony with commitment to partnership and equity

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So in closing,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I believe this executive budget reflects an important investment in children, youth, and families and communities across New York City,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: and it represents an opportunity to continue shaping systems that are not only effective, but also compassionate, responsive, and grounded in partnership.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: As I begin in this role,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I do so with humility, urgency, and hope.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I know the responsibility that comes with

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: with leading this agency, and I know the profound impact our decisions have on the lives of children and families.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I also know this work cannot be done by any one agency or system alone.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: It requires partnership across government with providers,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: advocates, communities, and with our partners in the judiciary, and most importantly, with young people and families whose lived experience must continue to inform and shape our work.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: At the same time, we are navigating a period of significant change and uncertainty nationally.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Federal policy shifts and broader pressures on social service systems continue to impact child welfare and youth serving systems across the country.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: But even as systems adapt, transform, and respond to those pressures, our responsibility must remain clear.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Children, youth, and families must be at the center of every decision we make.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: This moment calls not only for responsiveness, but for intentional transformation rooted in equity,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: community partnership, accountability, and the belief that every child and family deserves dignity, support, stability, and possibility.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I believe deeply in the power of connection and intentional action.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I believe children, youth, and families deserve systems that see their humanity,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Honor their strengths and create real pathways to healing, stability, opportunity, and belonging.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And I believe our collective responsibility is not simply to respond to crisis, but to help create conditions where children and families and communities can truly thrive.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: That's the work ahead of us, and I look forward to doing it in partnership with all of you.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Thank you, and I welcome your questions.

chapters.summary: Lee recognizes Councilmembers Marano, Ossé, Felder, Jay Sanchez, and Mealy who have joined. She notes both she and Stevens are former nonprofit leaders and emphasizes breaking government silos.
chapters.title: Chair Lee recognizes additional council members and begins questioning

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Before I go into my questions, just want to recognize we've been joined by Councilmember Marano on Zoom, Councilmembers Ossé, Councilmember Felder, Councilmember Jay Sanchez, and Councilmember Mealy.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And thank you so much, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We have two former nonprofit leaders up here, which really greatly appreciate the work and the portfolio that ACS has.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so I know that you are in charge of a lot, and there's a lot on your plate.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so one of the things I hate are silos in government.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so whatever we can do on the council, we've always tried to champion ways to make sure that the agencies are also talking to each other and ways that we can play a helpful role in that as well.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So thank you.

chapters.summary: Lee asks how ACS determined which savings to include and what metrics were used. Deputy Commissioner Pletnikoff explains the data-driven approach under Executive Order 12, focusing on operational efficiencies, program performance, and aligning budget to projected revenues.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions ACS on savings methodology and process

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And just wanted to, I have a few questions before I pass it on to the chair.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So in terms of the savings exercises, I know that every agency had a target goal for the FY27 executive plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: How did ACS determine which savings to include in the executive plan and what was the process in terms of what metrics were used to make the assessment?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: So as part of the Executive Order 12, all agencies were required to identify recurring savings

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: opportunities across their entire budget and review agency operations using a data-driven approach focused on operational efficiencies, program performance, and long-term sustainability.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: At ACS, we focused on aligning the budget to reflect our projected revenues,

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: took savings from sunsetting programs and contracts, and we also looked across the entire agency budget and evaluated contracts based on performance and agency need.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: I want to stress that these decisions were difficult and we approached them very thoughtfully.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: Our focus throughout the process was to find savings and efficiencies that would not eliminate services for children, youth, and families.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, because I know that when we talked about savings on the council side, and I know the speaker has said this as well, we really wanted to emphasize that we want to make sure that services are not cut.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so, you know, just wanting to make sure that that was actually the case, because you said sunsetting contracts and looking at other programs.

chapters.summary: Lee asks which programs and services were included in savings and whether any direct services see funding decreases. Pletnikoff explains savings came from maximizing revenue, sunsetting administrative contracts, IT savings, Esperanza closure, and slowing ramp-up of Fair Futures and Career Choice.
chapters.title: Chair Lee presses on which programs saw service decreases from savings

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so which programs and services, if at all, were included in the savings measures?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And will any direct services or programs see a decrease in funding from the savings exercise?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: So the majority of our savings initiatives actually came out of maximizing revenue.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: So more than half came through.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: realizing additional fringe that we were able to receive, as well as additional miscellaneous revenues out of audit.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: In terms of programs that were sunsetted, we sunsetted some administrative contracts.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: We made reductions for IT savings based on underspending in our Xerox contract.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: We had some programs that had already sunset, including Esperanza.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: We also looked at

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: looking at surpluses in our Committee on Special Education funding.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: We slowed down the ramp up of some programs that we had received funding as of last exec for our Youth Safety and Success Initiative, and that includes identifying

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: accrued savings in FY26 for the Career Choice Program, as well as slowing the ramp up of our Fair Futures Program.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: And then we also had savings through prevention contracts.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: We are ending four contracts for prevention.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: We believe there's still program capacity and so that all of the family and children will continue to be served.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And a lot of the slowing of the ramp up was because of capacity, hiring processes, that sort of thing, like to onboard.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know that my colleagues probably have more questions about some of those, so I'll save that for them.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about total loss of state and federal funding from savings and whether funding can be shifted. Pletnikoff explains revenue cannot be transferred because CTL dollars are needed to draw down revenue, and the total revenue takedown for FY27 is $29 million.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions state and federal funding losses from savings

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And several savings measures included a reduction of state and or federal funding.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And what is the total loss in funding from each state and federal source from savings?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And can any of this funding be shifted?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: To different programs?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: Yeah, so the revenue that was taken down is representative of the, as every CTL dollar we spend, we're able to draw down revenue.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: So the revenue can't really be transferred because we need the CTL dollar to draw it down.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: Give me one second and I'll tell you what the total value of the revenue taken down is.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: Give me two seconds.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: Oh, yeah, no worries.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: I can get it for you.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: I'm mostly focused on CTL, so I apologize.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: No worries.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I was going to try to ask a quick question, but I don't know if I don't want to disturb you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Please do.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Come back to it.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Because I'm bad at multitasking, so I never assume.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about average reimbursement timelines to providers. Pletnikoff reports 7.7 days for health and human service invoices, much lower than the citywide average of 17 days, and notes ACS leads in timely contract registration.
chapters.title: Chair Lee asks about contracting and provider reimbursement timelines

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So were you able to hit the target of the savings rate this year for the targeted amount, 1.5%?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It was more than that, right?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: The savings that we achieved was...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: At the target?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: Not at the Target, actually.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: And the total revenue takedown for FY27 is $29 million.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: $29 million, okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And the second part of the question was just about whether any of the funding could be shifted to different programs?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: No, okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, I'll probably have follow-up later on that, but moving on to contracting and procurement, my favorite topic.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Contracting is an issue across the board for a lot of city agencies, as we know, and

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What is the average timeline for reimbursements to providers at ACS?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: So the average process

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: Spending time for health and human service invoices is 7.7 days, which is much lower than the average across the city for health and human services agencies, which at the close of the year was 17.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: 7.7 days from the time they submit the invoice.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But then if there's questions about the invoicing and there's back and forth, like, is that, how long does that, and this is assuming the contracts are registered.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: We also lead agencies in timely contract resident registration, so we're very good there.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: We don't actually, I don't have a way to measure if there's back and forth in terms of

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: Questions that might come up if something is submitted incorrectly and it has to be resubmitted or sent back to the provider.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: We don't have a way of measuring that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: If there's some way to measure that, that'd be great, only because we've been hearing, and I say this also as someone that used to contract with DYCD, DIFTA,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: DOHMH, and a lot of other city agencies, but I know that the timeline it takes usually for the reimbursement once you submit the voucher is usually a bit longer.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Just wanted to know if you have any information on that, that would be great.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: Not to brag, but ACS is really good at this.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, yeah?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So you guys are like A+.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: And so I think it really is 7.7 days from when in total.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: That's why I'm skeptical a little bit because

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: From our nonprofit background, it takes a lot longer.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: They actually do well.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, that's good.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We want to know why other agencies aren't doing what they do.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah, exactly.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about contracting issues and provider concerns. Pletnikoff reports close to 100% of contracts registered on time on the human service side, with contracts typically registered within a couple months of Schedule C.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions ACS on contracting concerns and provider feedback

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And how has ACS dealt with and responded to challenges related to if there are any contracting issues or procurement issues on the process?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And what are major concerns or areas of concern for providers in terms of the contracting process?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Have you heard of any and gotten feedback?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: My understanding is that we have close to 100%, if not 100% of all of our contracts registered on time on the human service side.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: So I don't think we've been hearing those types of things.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So then once it gets awarded, Schedule C comes out and the contracts get procured, usually in a couple months it's usually registered with ACS?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about conversations with OMB regarding applying for the state hardship waiver. Pletnikoff confirms conversations have occurred but the decision rests with City Hall and OMB. ACS estimates $549M in expenses and $133M in capital spent on Raise the Age since FY19.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions Raise the Age waiver status and expenses

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, moving on to raise the age waiver.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: At the prelim budget hearing, we urged ACS to apply for the state for the hardship waiver because this is money that has been owed to the city, I think, since going back to 2019.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So since the preliminary budget hearing, have you had any conversations with OMB regarding applying for the waiver or pursuing state reimbursement?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: Yes, we have had those conversations.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: We appreciate the council's commitment and interest in New York City getting the funding from the state that it should get from Raise the Age.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: Back in the day, both Nancy and I advocated for Raise the Age and for the city to get reimbursement.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: And we've had those conversations with OMB.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: The decision about whether or not to seek a hardship waiver would not be up to ACS.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: It would be up to City Hall and OMB.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: And the funding for Raise the Age would be bigger than just ACS.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So, okay, that's kind of leading to my next question, which is, are you able to share information regarding how the expenses, I know, like you just mentioned, it wouldn't be just with ACS, but

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: would you be able to share any information regarding where the expenses would be directed?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: So in theory, ACS, probation, Mach J are all agencies and probably a couple others.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: NYPD and law.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and do you know if the mayor's office or if ACS would be able to share any of that information in terms of the funding?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: Sure, we shared a chart which I can send to you as part of our response to the preliminary budget.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: And it's an in-depth chart, so I can send that to you.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about $194.5M in baseline state funding and $300M total across agencies. Pletnikoff says no further guidance from the state yet, with the budget possibly being signed during the hearing. The funding is meant as revenue, not expansion.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions at-risk youth state funding plans

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and can you state for the record just how much you estimate the agency has spent on the implementation of Raise the Age from fiscal 2019 to date, which I'm assuming is quite a bit.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: Yes, we estimate that we have spent, going back to FY19, $549 million in expense and $133 million in capital.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And for at-risk youth funding, we had asked at the preliminary budget if you had details regarding the $194.5 million in baseline state funding provided,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: which was part of the total of $300 million added across several agencies that the state had given.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So since the prelim budget hearing, have you received any further guidance or clarification from the state?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: We have not, although we're hoping it will be imminent because I believe the governor is probably signing the budget while we're sitting here.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: But it did finally pass legislature last night, so hopefully we'll be able to get more details soon.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And if you could let us know which programs and services it'll fund, that would be great for follow-up.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And the $300 million is definitely a significant portion of funding for the new fiscal year.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And have you developed any plans for how it will utilize the funding for FY27 and beyond?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Have those conversations happened?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Not yet.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: Not to my awareness.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So for follow-up, if you could let us know which programs you're thinking of expanding, reworking, adjusting, that would be great.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: My understanding about the $300 million is it's not meant for expansion, but meant as revenue.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Revenue.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: If you could, yeah, either way, that would be great if you could follow up.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I think that's it for now, but I'm going to pass it over to Chair Stevens.

chapters.summary: Ossé raises concerns about families in his district unable to access non-mandated child care vouchers. Deputy Commissioner Volcomore explains the state budget determines funding for low-income vouchers, currently no new vouchers can be issued, but recertifications continue and families are directed to NYC Public Schools and DYCD alternatives.
chapters.title: Councilmember Ossé questions ACS on non-mandated child care vouchers

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Before I go, Councilmember Osei asked if he could ask a question because he had to run.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I hope you got your question.

utterances.speaker: Chi A. Ossé
utterances.text: Thank you so much, chairs.

utterances.speaker: Chi A. Ossé
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Chi A. Ossé
utterances.text: Nice to see you.

utterances.speaker: Chi A. Ossé
utterances.text: There are members of my district who are concerned about the issue of non-mandated child care vouchers for working parents.

utterances.speaker: Chi A. Ossé
utterances.text: These are vouchers for low-income families who do not qualify for benefits but do not earn enough to afford child care.

utterances.speaker: Chi A. Ossé
utterances.text: Because they were told the city has no more funding for these vouchers, parents and families in my district are concerned because these vouchers are crucial for them accessing child care.

utterances.speaker: Chi A. Ossé
utterances.text: I would like to know what are the administration's plans for funding these vouchers, and if there is a funding gap, what would be the alternative for these families?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Well, first of all, I'll just acknowledge that child care is absolutely a critical priority for the Mamdani administration and for us.

utterances.speaker: Chi A. Ossé
utterances.text: I would hope so.

utterances.speaker: Chi A. Ossé
utterances.text: It would be crazy if you said otherwise, right?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I would actually like to turn it over to Deputy Commissioner Volcomore to be able to give some details about what the plans are.

utterances.speaker: Chi A. Ossé
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: Yeah, absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: So you're right, and as Stephanie said, we understand the budget is being finalized as we speak, so we're eager to see what is in the state budget because it is state funding that supports those low-income vouchers.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: It's true that currently we are not able to issue new vouchers, though we continue to recertify families that have vouchers.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: So the vast majority of families using vouchers are able to hang on.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: As soon as the budget is finalized, we will analyze what that means for how we can move forward in terms of when we may be able to move the wait list that we are maintaining.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: Families, depending on age, have a few options.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: New York City public schools for children 0 to 5 do have open seats, and of course that depends on location and what families'preferences look like.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: And DYCD also has after-school programming, and we are regularly reaching out to families that are on our wait list to make sure that they are aware of those opportunities and can avail themselves of those.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Stevens expresses alarm at 362 youth in secure detention, up from 150 when she took office. She asks about interagency efforts to reduce numbers. Deputy Commissioner Ginsberg confirms active conversations with MACJ and DYCD, and Stevens demands a coordinated plan.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens questions ACS on 362 youth in secure detention

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you for being here.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And as I'm listening to your testimony and going through some of the things, one of the things that probably to me is very glaring as of May 13, 2026, that we have 362 young people in secure detention.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: This is like really alarming for me.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And so I think for me, my question is, and this is why I say it's so important for us to invest in our young people on the front end, because now we have 362 young people in secure detention.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And that's a huge alarming number for me.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: So I want to ask, and specifically, is there some type of interagency?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Task force or working group that's going on now to think about how do we reduce these numbers because

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: at this point I've been screaming at the top of my lungs for the last two years about this and I know everyone is up in arms about child care but I want us to be just as up in arms about 362 young people being in secure detention.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: This is alarming and honestly unacceptable to me so I would love to hear is there a plan and as we're going through this budget exercise and thinking about you know where savings are are we thinking about how to get these young people from not ending up here

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: and what would that look like

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Stevens.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Just to note that we absolutely share the value of and the importance of our young people.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: in their development and their well-being and absolutely crucial in focusing on what we're doing, both from a prevention of foster care involvement to also

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: prevention of involvement in the juvenile system.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I'll turn it over to Deputy Commissioner Ginsburg to give some more specific details.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: Thank you for your question.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: As you know, we share your concerns.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: I know.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: But it's just frustrating because you guys get them at the end.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And so that's why I said, is there like an interagency conversation on like how we're going to reduce these numbers?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Because you can't do this on your own, right?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Like how are we working with DOE and DYCD and all these agencies that work with young people to get this number down?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Because honestly, when we came in, we were at, I believe it was like 150.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And no one is talking about these numbers constantly increasing and it's really driving me crazy.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And so if we aren't doing that, I want to just stay on record that that is something that I need and want to see.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Really quick, soon.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: So the good news is that since the change of administration, we have been actively in conversation with MACJ.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: We are speaking with DYCD.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: We have always been speaking with DYCD.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: As you are aware, DYCD funds many of our programs in the facilities and also funds programs in the community.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: So when young people leave us, they can continue with some of that programming as

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: in the communities in order to prevent them from coming back to us.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: Oh, I know.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: We're seeing them later at 1, so they'll be getting the same question.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: That's why I have you guys all here, because I want us to make sure we're looking at this in a holistic perspective, because this is a huge concern of mine.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: So definitely want us to keep talking and thinking about how are we looking and being intentional about getting these numbers down, because this to me is unacceptable.

chapters.summary: Stevens expresses frustration that ACS reduced close-to-home capacity through the RFP and now needs to expand. Pletnikoff clarifies it's converting an existing 6-bed non-secure detention to 9 beds, not building a new facility. Stevens argues providers warned against right-sizing.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens questions close-to-home facility expansion after right-sizing

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Another thing in the testimony that was kind of glaring to me and was like,

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: something that I think is also connected to even the savings.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I see now that some of this money is going to be around securing a nine-bed non-secure placement facility for closed-to-homes.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: This is also super glaring and frustrating to me because when we did the RFP, we were right-sizing this program.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And I remember providers screaming from the top of their lungs that we are not right-sizing and that these numbers are going up and we shouldn't be closing any of them.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And they also said that it was going to be really hard, like once they close, that they would now have to say to get a facility back up and running, it takes a lot.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And we know that it's not something that can be done overnight.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: So this is frustrating that now we're building another facility.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And this is a case where we should be listening to providers.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And I get it.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Yes, we have to have savings, but it cannot be in areas that young people are impacted.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And so I need to understand how.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: How do we get back to a place if we were right-sizing now having to build a new facility?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: I want to just say, first of all, you are correct.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: The history is that we reduced close-to-home capacity as part of the RFP.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: I do want to mention that it's not building a new facility.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: It's a current six-bed non-secure detention that we're going to turn into a nine-bed non-secure placement.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: So it's already a site part of our juvenile justice continuum.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
