utterances.text: Okay, I just wanted to say again that the...

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: I know that doesn't get to the heart of your matter.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Yeah, it doesn't.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: But, you know, I just have to say the things, and it needs to be said on record, and it needs to be said because this was something in a place where providers said, hey, don't do this because we're going to need it.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And I feel like I'm going to go full circle to now where we're at with the savings and prevent it.

chapters.summary: Stevens argues prevention services were credited for reducing facility numbers, making cuts contradictory. Deputy Commissioner Linares explains the decision was difficult, based on capacity analysis, SCR costs, and provider performance against KPIs across FY24-FY26.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens challenges prevention service cuts as contradictory to prior claims

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Preventive care.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And this is also frustrating because for the last two years, because I've only had ACS for two years, the last two years it was said that the reason that these numbers are down in these facilities is because of the work of preventive care and that that was necessary.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I don't want to get y'all in trouble because I be doing whatever and I don't know how Councilmember Lee does her thing.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Y'all know I be clapping.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: My people don't know how to behave.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: But it gets me very frustrated because preventative services has been toted as a reason for

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: not having so many people on

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: in the facility.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I think in your testimony you said that we're down like 13,000 from a decade ago and like these numbers are consistently going down and it has been a continuous effort to get

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: these numbers down and prevent preventive services has been like the thing that has been pointed to of saying we're going to get these things down.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And so how do we now sit here and say and justify that it's services, that this is a place that services should have savings?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Because I know we don't like to use cuts.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I know that's not what we're saying, right?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: But how do we justify that now when that has been the thing that we've pointed to?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And so what is the plan to ensure that the continuity stays?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Because one of the things that I think for me is frustrating is that

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I've heard things like, well, there's still slots.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And so if I'm a young person, well, if I'm a family and there's slots in

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: the Bronx and I live in Queens and I already don't like the system, because let's be clear, and I already have issues, I'm not going.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: So I'm trying to understand what the justification is, what's the plan, and how this is going to look in actual time for these families.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: So I'll stop chatting.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So I'll start out with, and this might be my own artificial separation.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: You're welcome.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Hello.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: The close to home related to the work of trying to support young people that are involved with juvenile justice.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And those placements and then the prevention services connected to preventing foster care placement are kind of aligned but different sort of yeah they're all different right

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: so I was just trying to make was like we need to listen to providers because they said this and now again we need to absolutely and the partnership of providers is critical and you heard that in my testimony

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: and prevention services are critical as well the entire continuum we also as an agency have to have responsibility for you know fiscal responsibility and aligning resources in

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: in ways so that we are investing and utilizing the the resources we do have and the decisions to eliminate slots is a really difficult decision and I'm happy to turn it over to

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner Linares to talk a little bit more about the prevention programs that I think you're referring to.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: Chair Stevens.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: So I share your sentiment.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: It was a very difficult decision for us, but we really had to maintain our fiscal responsibility and ensure that we are maintaining a strong prevention program as possible.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: We have looked at different indicators to make the recommendations.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: We wanted to make sure that one of the first things that we considered when thinking about this savings was our family is going to continue to have services.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: So you're right, we don't want a family from Brooklyn to be thinking about a service in the Bronx.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks for details on the four programs being eliminated. Linares confirms three in Brooklyn and one in Queens, totaling 360 slots with 199 active families currently receiving services. Stevens presses on proximity of alternative services for affected families.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens asks about locations of four prevention programs being cut

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And a couple of the programs actually were in Brooklyn that's being cut, correct?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Can you talk about the four programs?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Can you tell us what's the total number of contracts that are under this program that are being eliminated and where they're located?

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: So before I answer that, I wanted to mention that when we look at

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: Things like families in the Bronx versus Brooklyn, we were very intentional in thinking about where our capacity was.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: So we try to have a bird's eye view of our entire system before we think about where to make a recommendation.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: Once we did that, then we landed in the catchment that we did, which are three programs in Brooklyn and one in Queens.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: Once we determined

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: We looked at things like, are there other providers?

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: So it was one in Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: Three in Brooklyn and one in Queens.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: Once we looked at the catchment and determined that these were the areas where we had the more capacity, where we had less

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: SCR costs compared to other areas, where we have other prevention providers available in those areas.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: Once we did that, then we narrowed down to those areas.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: Then we said, okay, so now let's look at the providers that are in the areas and let's think about their own performance against the key performance indicators that we have identified.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: And those are utilization, the number of families that they served.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: And we didn't look at a point in time, so we didn't look just at today, how you were doing, but we were intentional in looking at fiscal year 24, fiscal year 25, and we also looked at the remaining

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: what's passed in fiscal year 26.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: So we wanted to have a very comprehensive understanding of how those programs were faring against those KPIs.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: The other thing that we had to consider was whether those programs were serving a special population.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: Once we did that, then we brought it down to those four providers that were put forward as recommendations.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: How many clients are served by those programs?

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: How many?

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: So in family support programs, we serve citywide a total of 4,512.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: As of April, we had a total of 3,347 families that were receiving, participated in services or family support.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: For these four programs, we have a total of 360 slots.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: Currently active in those active families that are actually receiving services is 199.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: In those programs.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: In those programs.

chapters.summary: Stevens expresses concern about vulnerable families, particularly Black and brown families, having to travel for services and trust new social workers. Linares details the transition process including dedicated case review teams, provider coordination, and family involvement in next-step decisions.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens questions transition plan for families losing prevention providers

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Has there been an analysis on, like, where is the closest one, like, if those families are not being served there, where is the closest facility or place they would have to go?

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: So that was part of the metrics that we looked when making a decision or a recommendation because we wanted to make sure that families can have services.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: Again, that was our first beginning point.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: Will families continue to have services?

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: Not only the family that are currently in the program.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Well, I know they'll be able to still have services, but I'm trying to understand, like,

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: what is like the distance like how like especially like prime example you said there's three in brooklyn where those are are those in the same area and then were there other programs like nearby

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: um because again like the we know that these are vulnerable families

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: We know that these are a lot of black and brown families.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I literally have a resolution about the harm that AC has done to black and brown families because we know of the past harm.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And so this to me is a disinvestment in those communities because we know we don't want to.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: You know, criminalized poverty.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And a lot of these cases are often around poverty.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And so like, if I'm already struggling and now I have to like travel and have a burden, like I'm trying to get an assessment of like, what's the closest facility that they are going to have to go to?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Have you guys already thought about like what that transition plan is?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Like a lot of these families are vulnerable.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I don't, I barely wanted to talk to this social worker.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Now you think I'm gonna talk to a new social worker?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I'm not doing it.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: So I'm trying to get a better understanding of what that's going to look like and understanding like the bigger impact of like how like and again I want to be clear I know that no one wants to ever have programs cut and I know that you guys don't want

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: to have things cut but I just need us to make sure as a city we're prioritizing different things and for me prioritization is young people and this is a place and families of like there's going whether we like to say it or not there's going to be harm

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And for me, it's like, how are we mitigating this and how are we moving thoughtfully?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And I'm not saying that you're not, but what I'm saying is this is my concern.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: This is why I'm focusing on this, because there is no way that families who are already vulnerable, families who already don't trust the system, are now going to say, all right, well, it's slots over here, so I'm going to go.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: So I just am trying to have more of a concrete of like, like,

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: What does this transition look like?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: What is the plan?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And how are we going to ensure that we are mitigating the harm of all these families?

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: I like to tell you that unfortunately for me, I have lots of experience on ending contracts because of the end of contracts in 2020 when we transitioned the system to the no RFP.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: During that time, we had about 5,000 families or so that we were transitioning and that we did so successfully.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: I like to say that we have very carefully thought about what would it mean for each individual families.

chapters.summary: Stevens challenges the 90-day transition period as too short for both families and workers losing jobs. Commissioner Jones-Gaston references successful transition of 5,000 families during COVID in six months. Stevens calls the response cold and advocates for protecting human service workers.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens questions 90-day transition timeline and impact on workers

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: You should know that as part of the end of contract, immediately as the providers are notified, we have a team specifically dedicated to reviewing each and every case

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: family that is involved actively in prevention.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: The decision about the next steps are made together with the provider, ACS, and the family.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: That is that if the family decides that this is a time when a case can close and we can determine that that's an appropriate decision, then we go ahead and make a case closure.

utterances.speaker: Louisa Linares
utterances.text: So it is...

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: It's a process that is very well thought out and one that I'm personally very proud of, despite the fact that it means that we are ending a contract.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: What were these contracts in?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So, um...

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I'm going to ask Margaret to help me with that question.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Because I...

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Savings were projected for FY27.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: We know that we need at least 90 days to continue the case management to transfer cases.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And so...

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So we're saying 90 days.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: We know that we need 90 days from the beginning of the implementation in order to transfer all cases.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I should say that we transferred 5,000 families in a six-month period during COVID, and we did so successfully.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So while 90 days seems like a very short time, I do believe that we have a dedicated team that will make sure that every family and providers get the support that they need to make this transition.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I mean, I just...

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Yes, the families is also a factor in this too, but 90 days for folks who are now out of a job is also a huge factor.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: So we can't pretend like that's not part of the conversation.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Like I don't, I agree.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: It kind of felt cold, so.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: It's not.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I'm sorry, guys.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: This ain't my hearing.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Don't worry.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: When we go back to school, we'll be crazy.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: But sorry, guys.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: But that's kind of upsetting.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And, you know, this budget isn't complete.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: So my hope is that we come up with a better solution than 90 days of thinking about the transition of families and figuring out a better way to have savings because, again.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Everybody's talking about an affordable city on both sides.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And that don't sound like we are thinking about workers, right?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I feel like we've passed multiple bills around workers in this council.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: So my hope is that my colleagues get along boards for us to fight to make sure that these workers are also protected.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Because the human service sector.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: keeps getting hit.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: So, you know, I hope other, you know, folks get on board with this.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Because that is kind of heartbreaking to think that people will be losing their jobs in 90 days.

chapters.summary: Stevens questions why Fair Futures faces $4.9M in baseline savings when ACS has touted the program's success. Deputy Commissioner Mendez explains the savings slow the ramp-up timeline but the program still grows, reaching 4,800 in FY27 and 5,400 by FY29, with more money per slot.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens questions Fair Futures funding reduction rationale

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I have, I'm going to transition to some other questions around fair futures.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I'm going to do some questions and I'll go to colleagues and then we'll come back.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: The executive plan included baseline savings of $4.9 million from the city and state funding, stating that fiscal 2026 related to fair futures programs after speaking with

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: OMB understood that the savings should only been reflected in fiscal 2026 through 2028 that is expected to restore the funding in fiscal 2029 and 2030 in the future fiscal plan.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: ACS has publicly stated that they desire to expand fair futures programs.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Giving this, what is the rationale for the funding's reduction within fair futures?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And this is, and I bring this up again, this is another program that ACS has been raising the flag about and saying how great it is and all the things.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: So I get that we are in savings, but it is mind boggling that the programs that we've talked about for the last two years since I've been here and pointed to the success of getting these numbers down and foster care and transitioning that this is where we're finding savings.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: So I just got to say it.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So I'll just start and then turn over to Deputy Commissioner Mendez.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: You know, I think these programs are exactly what you're referring to when we're talking about

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: about focusing on youth and well-being and our responsibility to helping them develop and become successful adults and understand how to navigate the world as best as possible.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And our commitment to that continues.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And you'll hear from the deputy commissioner that it actually is not a loss in services and the investment is intended to be used in full.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Thank you for your question.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So regarding fair futures, you may recall in fiscal year, I believe it was 23, we baseline $30 million.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: The city baseline $30 million in the program.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: We were allowed to expand up to 26, serving children in care and out of care.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: And then with the Youth Safety and Success Initiative, we received additional funding because we wanted to make sure there were additional resources because as we support the kids in care, even though that number is going down, as children are leaving, we wanted to continue.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So there was a plan, a robust plan, to take these additional dollars and have the agencies quickly ramp up to serve.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: I think the original plan was 5,300 children starting either in fiscal year 27 or fiscal year 28.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So what we're doing is we're still expanding, and by fiscal year 29, 28, one of those fiscal years, 29, we'll get to 5,400.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: But we are doing this incrementally, and we're allowing the...

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: the providers to have a little bit more time to ramp up, and that will help them build the infrastructure, do the onboarding, and hire the additional staff.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So in fact, in fiscal year 27, they are receiving more money per slot for Fair Futures, and we are looking for them to grow.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So the target, I don't have my glasses on, but there was a target for this year, you know how I am, and then there's going to be a target for fiscal year 27.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So they are going to be growing, reaching more young people.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: The same thing for fiscal year 28.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: And then by fiscal year 29, they should have their infrastructure and all of their additional staff in place to reach that 5,400 number at a minimum.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: What I also will say is if providers come to us and say, look, I can do more than what you're asking us to do.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: For example, we have the capacity to look at resource and shift so that no one will, if there's additional children that need fair futures, we're committed to figuring out how they can get the supports because we are committed to the program.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks how ACS communicates to providers that they can request additional funding. Mendez explains the contract management unit works with providers on budgets and will ensure agencies know they can discuss additional needs. Commissioner Jones-Gaston commits to creating new provider feedback mechanisms.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens asks how providers learn about additional Fair Futures funding availability

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So we are growing.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: We're just kind of.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Creating a more of a runway to that growth.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And even to your point there around, like providers can come if they need additional growth, because I know this was a conversation we were having with the last commissioner where providers weren't coming and saying that they needed additional

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: funding or supports or whatever.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: How are we communicating that to providers?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Because that is one of the things that providers are saying that they didn't know even was possible.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So within the Division of Family Permanency,

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: We have a contract management unit, and they work with the providers around their budgets.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So the providers are very vocal when they're having questions or they want to figure out the budget.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So that is that conference, that conversation, and we work on their budget.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: We look at their budget narrative.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So I will make sure the team communicates to each agency because we go over each of their budgets that if you feel you need more for fair futures or for anything, let's talk so that we can make sure that they can.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Soundly do the work that they need to do.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Sorry, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I just wanted to add, you know, in my role, I intend to have

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: different mechanisms for being able to spend time and have conversations and hear from providers in a variety of ways, in addition to each of the various programmatic areas and their relationship and contact on the nitty-gritty of the contract.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: That is one of the commitments I've made.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: That's good to hear.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks about specific targets and benchmarks for Fair Futures. Mendez details targets of 4,800 for FY27, 5,000+ for FY28, and 5,400 for FY29, with current data showing 4,300 youth served, 87% in care, and two-thirds of eligible youth reached. Stevens raises concerns about inconsistent information sharing with youth.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens questions Fair Futures targets and utilization metrics

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: What are some of the targets around, because I know you spoke about the different target areas around the fair futures.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: What are those different target areas?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And also thinking about, I know as the commissioner is coming in and you're saying you're going to talk with them, do you guys have benchmarks of triggers like, okay, we see that you're increasing and so how do we then

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: you know, have that turnaround and just go over the targets.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So for fiscal year 27, the target would be about 4,800.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: For fiscal year 28, we

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: want to be at 5,000, a little over 5,000, and again in fiscal year 29 at that 5,400.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: We are in regular conversation with the Center for Fair Futures because they do a lot of the training and help the agencies implement the model.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: They have a database.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Our data team is able to look at data.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: And what we really do want to focus on now as we're moving into this second piece of the expansion is setting up some metrics and holding agencies accountable.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: We were able to pull some data, and so we're able to say that right now, there's about 4,300 young people involved with Fair Futures this fiscal year so far.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: It looks like, I don't want to jinx anything, that we might be on track to surpass what we did last year, but 87% of those kids are in care, 13% are...

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: No longer in care and we're reaching them.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: And then we know about two thirds of young people that are eligible are getting fair futures.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So how do we make that number?

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: How do we get to 75%?

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: How do we get to 80%?

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: And I think that's working with the Center for Fair Futures and also the agencies and having specific metrics that we can touch base on.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: We have a team in the Office of Youth Safety and Success.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So we were gonna have a conversation and kind of co-design that with the providers so that we can make sure everyone is reaching these targets that we've outlined.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Yeah, and I would love for us to continue to think about what that looks like even around the targets because, again, and thank you guys for coming to Shadow Day for our foster care youth here.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Thank you for having us.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Yeah, they were really excited.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: to have you guys in the information, but even some of that, and I'm not sure, they didn't ask you guys a lot of questions, but some of the conversations I had with them in passing, there's still a big inconsistency of what information is being shared according to agencies.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: And so definitely want to make sure that we're working on that, especially with a program like this and how do we reach those targets?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Because if young people don't know, they don't know.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks how often the contract management unit meets with providers and how utilization is tracked. Mendez explains monthly invoice reviews, conversations about vacancies and turnover, and plans to refine qualitative tracking alongside monetary monitoring.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens asks about contract management frequency and provider utilization tracking

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Is a lower number of young people than originally planned for being served by the program?

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: What specific steps is ACS taking to ensure providers are fully utilizing the funding in this program so that all eligible young people are receiving those vital supports?

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So again, we have our contract management unit.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: And in addition to, they're not just...

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: How often do they meet with the providers of the contract management unit?

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: We need to get back to you with an exact often, but like invoices come in monthly or, you know, within a certain, like a few times a quarter.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: And there's a lot of conversations with those, you know, the finance teams at the agencies.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Now agencies have their allocations, at least for enhanced family foster care and specialized family foster care.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: We're going to be looking for them to submit the budget.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So there'll be a lot of conversations now.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So I'll get an exact number I can get back to you.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: I think I forgot your question, though.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: What did you ask me?

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: I know.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Your original question.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: We were just asking about, like, the utilization of the program.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Oh, yes.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So they submit, you know, and so we're able to look at the tracking and, like, basically on what they're submitting.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So let's say, you know, they're budgeted for X and then.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So far, they've submitted a number, you know, much lower.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: We're going to have conversations about what's happening.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Is there like a trigger where it's like, okay, you don't see a number and you guys can reach out and say like, what supports do you need?

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Exactly.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So that's the role of the contract management team is to look at like, hey, you haven't submitted invoices.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Are there vacancies?

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Are there staff turnover?

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Are there difficulties?

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: I think we want to refine and hone in.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: More around the qualitative piece, not just look at the monetary piece, but the qualitative piece and how are you leveraging that information.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: And then I think our partnership with the Center for Fair Futures will help intensify because they're working with the coaches regularly.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So it's really just about expanding and making sure new staff come on and they say, oh, you're this age, you should go to Fair Futures, let me make a referral, that type of thing.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Chair Stevens, just to add, you know, one of the things that I have throughout my career enjoyed is being able to spend time with young people and hear directly from them.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And so as I'm learning all of the parts and pieces that we have available in ACS, certainly wanting to ensure that we've got pathways for young people to engage and give us feedback directly

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: about what they need and how communication and information is best communicated to them from the various agencies that are serving them.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And so I'm looking forward to engaging with the young people around that issue as well.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And thank you for having a shadow day.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I think it's critical.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Well, if you...

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: You know me, and everybody knows this.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I only like kids, and I get all the information from them, which is why when I come back, I'm so passionate because I am translating.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I don't really like the adult.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: But the young people are what I'm really focused on, and so a lot of the things that I'm coming back with is direct conversations that I have with them because I think it's so important that they have access to government and have conversations with them.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: So I look forward to going out and hanging out with kids and changing the system to actually work for them.

chapters.summary: Wong asks about the ACS headquarters move. Deputy Commissioner Glendell explains all staff are moving from 150 Williams Street to 110 Williams Street by end of June, with the new location designed to be more family-friendly with meeting rooms and playrooms.
chapters.title: Councilmember Wong questions ACS headquarters relocation

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: I still have a ton of questions, but I'm going to pass it to Chair Lee so that our colleagues could ask some questions and come back to me on the second round.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We've been joined by Councilmember Ressler, Majority Leader Abreu, Councilmember Gutierrez, and we are going to start off with Councilmember Wong, followed by Councilmember Shulman.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I would like to start about the relocation of your headquarters.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: When did the process begin and what is, do you have an update on that?

utterances.speaker: Stephanie Glendell
utterances.text: As the last sole employee at 150 Williams Street, I'll try to answer that question.

utterances.speaker: Stephanie Glendell
utterances.text: So sad.

utterances.speaker: Stephanie Glendell
utterances.text: We are, by the end of May, all staff, or by the end of the day today, all staff will have moved out of 150 Williams Street.

utterances.speaker: Stephanie Glendell
utterances.text: I got special exemption to stay through the hearing.

utterances.speaker: Stephanie Glendell
utterances.text: And by the end of June, we'll be fully out of 150 Williams Street.

utterances.speaker: Stephanie Glendell
utterances.text: Our new headquarters is at 110 Williams Street.

utterances.speaker: Stephanie Glendell
utterances.text: It's not fully complete.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Can you explain about the move?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Like what's, is there something, is there issues with the current headquarters?

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Please talk about it.

utterances.speaker: Stephanie Glendell
utterances.text: So we had been in the current headquarters for about 30 years.

utterances.speaker: Stephanie Glendell
utterances.text: The new headquarters is a much more family, it has been intentionally developed to be more family friendly and to better meet the needs of the work we do.

utterances.speaker: Stephanie Glendell
utterances.text: So there are a lot of

utterances.speaker: Stephanie Glendell
utterances.text: family meeting rooms.

utterances.speaker: Stephanie Glendell
utterances.text: There are like a beautiful new playroom for kids.

utterances.speaker: Stephanie Glendell
utterances.text: The new office of advocacy is much more family friendly.

utterances.speaker: Stephanie Glendell
utterances.text: It was just developed in a different time.

utterances.speaker: Stephanie Glendell
utterances.text: And so I

utterances.speaker: Stephanie Glendell
utterances.text: don't know all the details.

utterances.speaker: Stephanie Glendell
utterances.text: I wasn't personally involved in the details to relocate, but have been part of some of the, like, it wasn't ULERP, but the city planning process.

utterances.speaker: Stephanie Glendell
utterances.text: So I know a bit about sort of the idea was that this new location would better meet our needs for staff and for families.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

chapters.summary: Wong asks about youth numbers at Horizon and Crossroads detention centers. Ginsberg reports 181 at Horizon and 186 at Crossroads including 11 young women. Wong raises a state comptroller report on 13-73% absenteeism; Ginsberg says current attendance is 72-74%, close to community rates.
chapters.title: Councilmember Wong questions detention center populations and school absenteeism

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I would like to ask about the two detention centers.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I've been reading the mayor's executive budget.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I've been hearing and reading the statement.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: I just don't see any numbers regarding the number of...

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Kids in the two sites at the Brownsville site, 17 Bristol Street and the one in Mott Haven, 560 Brook Avenue.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Can you talk about like?

utterances.speaker: Stephanie Glendell
utterances.text: Sure, Nancy can give you, we have numbers in the testimony, but Nancy can give you what we have today.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: As of this morning, we had 181 young men in Horizon.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: In Crossroads, we had 186 young people.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: 11 are young women in that facility.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Last year, there's a report from the state comptroller, the Napoli, regarding both sites.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: And I'm going to read that to you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: The report was data in April 2025, and it mentions that the...

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: The absence rate, school absence, absenteeism rates ranges anywhere from 13 to 73% days absent.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Can you talk about what's been done to improve that?

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: Yes, I can talk about that.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: That report covered the period of COVID.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: It was a period of five years, mostly that predated the Adams administration.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: That's what it was looking at.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: The current attendance rate, according to our DOE partners in both sites, is about

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: 72 to 74%, which is close to the attendance rate in the community.

chapters.summary: Wong asks about average time in detention and graduation rates. Ginsberg reports 97.4 days average stay, about 150 graduates over 3.5 years, and a CUNY MOU enabling college programming through Hostos and BMCC for youth with diplomas or GEDs.
chapters.title: Councilmember Wong questions detention length of stay and graduation rates

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Can you talk about the number of days the child spends in a detention center on average before they get to leave?

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: The average length of stay in secure detention for 2025 was about 97.4 days.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: What about the graduation rates on these schools?

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: So graduation rates are difficult.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: The school program is a program.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: It's not a school because young people don't start on the first day of school.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: They come whenever the court mandates them into our facility.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: We have graduated about 150 young people over the last three and a half years.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: And two years ago, we signed an MOU with CUNY, and we have young people who have either graduated with a diploma

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: or have achieved their GED engaged in college programming in both facilities through Hostos and BMCC.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Phil Wong
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, I've been reminded we've got to try to stick to time as best as possible, but we're going to do round two questions as well.

chapters.summary: Schulman highlights Forestdale in her district faces cuts and asks about losing the state match and whether other areas could be cut instead. Pletnikoff confirms 62% state reimbursement loss and notes over 1,000 vacant slots. Schulman implores reconsideration given prevention's long-term cost savings.
chapters.title: Councilmember Schulman questions prevention cuts and state match loss

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Shelman, followed by Councilmember Joseph.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Good morning, Commissioner, and welcome to New York.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I have to say your background is quite impressive, and we're lucky to have you as the ACS Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Since 1999, the number of children removed from families by ACS has dropped more than 80%

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: thanks to preventive services programs such as the one in my district at Forestdale, a 135-year-old organization that is the largest and most effective social services agency in the Borough of Queens.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: However, the families helped there are now at risk because of these

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: proposed cuts.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I want to ask you, well, first of all, I know that a lot of your background is in preventive services, and you've made some great achievements in many states and in the federal government.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So with that, I want to know, you know, this, the two things.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: One is the cuts that are being made to preventive services also have a match that you're losing, a state match that you're losing as part of that.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: That's number one.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Number two is that it represents a very small piece of the ACS budget.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So I'm wondering if there are other areas that can be cut instead.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And the next question after that is,

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Forestdale is getting the biggest cut, or it looks like it is.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: They're going to have to lay off some work from some employees.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And I have visited personally with the families there because I give them a lot of money from my budget.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And they have thanked me for keeping, you know, it's important to keep the families together and keep the children safe, which is your mantra.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So is there a way that we can do these cuts differently?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Thank you for the welcome.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: It is a pleasure to be here and an honor to serve.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I'll just say, yes, prevention services are absolutely and continue and will be a priority.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And you've heard in testimony today around some of the process and consideration that went into the decision making.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I have had a chance.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I haven't had a chance to visit any of the providers yet on day six.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: But look forward to getting to get out and meet with the providers, their teams, community members, and the families that are being served.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: For the specific question around match, I'm going to turn to Deputy Commissioner Plentnikov, and then if there's more questions about process for decision-making, happy to pull in

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner Linares as well.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair Member.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: So the cuts are actually pretty even across all four contracts.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: So it's approximately the same at all providers.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: The reimbursement is 62% that we received from the Child Welfare Services state funding.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Yeah, and I'm asking because the total amount of the cut for the preventive services is a small piece of ACS's budget.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So were there any other areas that...

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Especially since it is so important to have preventive services and we've seen the outcomes from preventive services.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: That's number one.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And number two is that if you cut and then you have to do remediation on the other end, that's even more expensive.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So that's what I'm asking here, if there's a way to do it differently.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I know the admin has made a decision.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I get that.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You know, the mayor ran on working with vulnerable families and also those that, you know, and the family unit.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And so I think it's just important to really push and ask that question.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: Yes, and I understand your concern.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: We did look across the entire agency, look to maximize revenues, look to find areas of underspending, and really kept our focus on the least impact to children, youth, and families.

utterances.speaker: Althea Stevens
utterances.text: We want to note that there are over 1,000 vacant slots in this program.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I understand.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I understand.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: There are reasons for vacant slots, depending on when the contract gets paid, depending on a whole bunch of stuff.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Commissioner, you were a social worker.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: You know what's involved sometimes with families and everything else.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: So I get that, but I'm imploring that this be looked at again.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I don't want to take up too much more time because I know that.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: My time is up.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And as a fellow social worker who ran a nonprofit, I totally just want to reiterate what Chair Stevens was saying about how prevention is so key.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It actually saves the city money in the long run.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I think it's smart to actually invest in more preventative services.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then also just wanted to reiterate that it really does impact just the planning.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I say this with, like, almost past trauma in my voice.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But, you know, it affects the planning of organizations, staffing, budgets, all of that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So, you know, we'll definitely be in continued conversation.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know there's going to be more questions about it, but I just wanted to reiterate that point.

chapters.summary: Joseph asks how the city plans to expand the initiative while cutting the New Visions portal it depends on. Deputy Commissioner Mendez explains ACS is working with NYC Public Schools on an MOU and cloud service agreement to introduce the portal to foster care agencies as a pilot for the next school year.
chapters.title: Councilmember Joseph questions Every Child and Family Is Known initiative and New Visions portal

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And we have Councilmember Joseph, followed by Councilmember Epstein.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you both chairs and I'd love to associate with your comments as well.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Welcome Commissioner, how are you?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: The Every Child and Family is known initiative, a collaborative between ACS and DOE which received additional funding in the same budget to expand its work with students living in shelters and other families facing crisis relies on the New Visions portal to track and respond to families'needs.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: This school year, over 50,000 check-ins have been logged by caring adults in the portable.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Please walk me through how does the city plan on expanding every child and family is known initiative while simultaneously cutting the platform it depends on to do the work.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Can you share if these cuts are sustained?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: How will it impact your work at ACS to support our youth who need this initiative the most?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Thank you for the question and thank you for the welcome.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I'm going to turn it over to Deputy Commissioner Mendez to be able to answer some of the specifics that you asked.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: But just again, reiterating, you know, our role when we're working with children and families is to ensure that they have their needs met and that we're finding ways to resource

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: their needs based on what they need and not just what we have.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And look forward to continuing to have conversations with you.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Our numbers are tremendous in New York City public school students in temporary housing.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: It's over 155,000 students.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And you know I know that work.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Morning.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Councilmember Joseph.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So I'm happy to report ACS is working very closely with New York City Public Schools, the foster care office.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: As you know, we have a close collaboration with them and new visions to explore how to introduce the access to that portal to foster care agencies.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So as you can imagine, there's a lot of things we have to do on the back end.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: We have to formalize an MOU.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Then we have to look at, I'm not the computer person, but I think it's called a cloud service agreement.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So we're going through some of those bureaucratic things to put in place, but we're also starting to talk to the foster care providers.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So we're thinking about doing this as a pilot first, and we're talking to the foster care providers about what should this look like, what makes sense, and all of those things while we're doing the MOU.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: pieces.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: And so we're hoping that that will continue to move forward so that in the next school year, this can move, you know, it will move forward.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: So the timeline is for September this coming, so you'll spend the summer training and transitioning this platform?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Is that what that's looking like?

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So that's the timeline.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: I think, you know, part of the, and I can get back to you with actual specific milestones, like I mentioned, we have to create this MOU.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So sometimes there's back and forth, I think.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Councilmember Lee talked about back and forth, so there'll be some back and forth.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Until you get it right.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Right, until we get it right.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: The cloud service agreement.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: I know New York City Public Schools is currently using New Vision, so that should not be a problem for ACS.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: They should pass all those cybersecurity tests.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: But I don't want to speak.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: I don't want to get ahead of myself.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: I do know we're trying to launch in the next school year.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So we'll continue to work on that.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: We get around to it, right, Shirley?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: I have more questions.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And I'll just commit that we can follow up as it's evolving with you directly.

chapters.summary: Epstein, a former foster care worker, asks about outreach to fill vacant prevention slots and plans for the Children's Center. Jones-Gaston commits to improving outreach. Mendez details robust services at the Children's Center including mental health, mentorship, and 39 current residents with reduced length of stay.
chapters.title: Councilmember Epstein questions prevention outreach and Children's Center services

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Epstein, followed by Councilmember Ressler, is he here?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: He's somewhere.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: We can go get him if you want.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: But Commissioner, welcome.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Just to disclose, I was a foster care worker back in the day, so I appreciate your commitment to this issue.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I do want to focus on the prevention.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: You know, as someone who worked in foster care,

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: You know, we're hearing a lot about the spaces, you know, vacant slots in prevention, but I want to talk about maybe the need to do additional outreach to prevent that to happen.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And maybe there's slots that are vacant is maybe related to the agency not doing a good enough job doing the outreach in communities to get people into those services.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: I'm wondering.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Obviously, you're new into this.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Kind of what commitment are you going to make to ensure that there's outreach into our community to make sure people know about those preventive services so they can get into those slots?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Thank you for the question and the welcome.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Yeah, as I am getting my feet underneath me and learning all the acronyms,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: One of the things I'm committed to is looking at all of the programs and processes that we have.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: have and really find ways that we can continue to improve and outreach and how people know about what's available and our relationships with providers and community are really, really critical to that.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Yeah, I would encourage you to do that and to work with us to ensure that happens because I do worry.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Then when you reduce prevention slots, then we will, you know, obviously we've heard a lot about this, the negative impact that it will have on families and, you know, the cost that it will, you know, obviously

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: the human cost to the city as well as the human cost to those families who aren't getting the support that they need to prevent bad things that happen to their families.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And I look forward to working with sister agencies also as collectively thinking about how are we serving kids and families as a whole.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: Yeah, I want to flag the Children's Center for you because this has been something, you know, before I came to this lovely body, I was an assembly member for eight years and dealt with lots of issues there and

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: insufficient programming and services available to the young people who are, some people are cycling in in a day, but some people are there for months on end.

utterances.speaker: Harvey D. Epstein
utterances.text: And I'm wondering what the plan is to ensure that the young people who are there are getting the support that they need while they're at the Children's Center.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I'm learning.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And one of the things I know is I agree with you.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: We absolutely, if children are

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: In our care, we have a responsibility to make sure that we're meeting their needs.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And working with Deputy, First Deputy Commissioner, I'm getting titles right, First Deputy Commissioner Saunders, who isn't here today, but with

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: understanding kind of what's happening at the Children's Center, where our needs are, and where there's opportunity for improvement.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Ultimately,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I would love that there's less and less need and utilization of the Children's Center

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: and that we have the ability to both keep children at home with their families and meet their needs, utilize kinship placements, identify

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: family home placements outside of having to use the Children's Center, but the quality of care is critical as well.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I'm going to turn it over to Deputy Commissioner Mendez if she has anything else to add.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So just to piggyback on your question regarding services, I won't go into detail.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: We can definitely share, but there's a lot of robust service, mental health and well-being services.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: JCCA provides mental health support, Children's Villages working with us on our youth that may be trafficked.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: We have different organizations providing mentorship program.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: We have an eight staff.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: ACS staff mental health team that's based in the Children's Center.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So there's a lot of programming that Commissioner Jones-Gaston mentioned that First Deputy Commissioner Wynette Saunders introduced.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: I'm happy to report today there are 39 young people at the Children's Center, and we have brought down considerable...

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: the length of time children stay there.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: There are some, yes, that stay older, our teens, but we're working with our foster care providers and also working with their families to see if they can safely be returned home.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So we've made a lot of strides.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: I could go on, but...

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Yeah, I'm happy to talk offline about that more because it's something that we've seen as an ongoing concern and problem.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Yes, and we work very closely with the 13% in the community district in that area on a regular basis.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So I think those partnerships and the commitment, while we don't want children staying at their children a long time while they are there, we want them to be supported and have their needs met.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I just wanted to add that First Deputy Commissioner Winnett Saunders really wanted to be here today and unfortunately had a medical appointment she couldn't change.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And so I just wanted to put that out for the record because I know she really would have wanted to share updated information about the Children's Center with you.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: As you know, we've both been longstanding members of the Children's Center and Community Advisory Board, and I'd be happy to set up a time for the three of us to meet so we can update you more fully.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Four of us, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Restler confirms 25,600 children on the voucher waitlist and presses on funding for FY27. Volcomore says the state budget must be analyzed first. Restler suggests SCCF vouchers as a one-year model and raises alarm about OCFS regulations limiting vouchers to exact work hours, which would disrupt child care centers.
chapters.title: Councilmember Restler questions voucher waitlist and OCFS hours policy

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We've been joined also by Councilmember Brewer, and we'll go on to Councilmember Ressler, followed by Gutierrez.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Congratulations, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: It's good to see the ACS team this morning.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I will just note, building on Councilmember Epstein for a second, the CAC in Brooklyn is moving.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We've received no

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: communication about that so would appreciate a briefing on what's planned and where that's going it's in our district it's an important location and we really want to make sure that that is handled thoughtfully

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: and with that I'm going to switch the topic that your team knows I'm going to ask about which is vouchers building on council member Ose's questions just to affirm there are 25,000 children currently on the

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Voucher waiting list?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So, first of all, absolutely commitment to follow up related to the CAC question that you have, and then I'll turn it over to Liz.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: Yes, based on the report from last Friday, 25,600.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: 25,600 children desperately in need of child care who do not have it today and with no plans right now to provide any funding to the families on the wait list.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: I want to be clear.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: I obviously share your concern and want to get care to as many families as possible.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: However, there are families on the wait list that are actively engaged in care, especially with New York City public schools.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: We keep those children on the wait list because if and when we are able to offer them a voucher, we want them to have the choice of type of care.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: You mentioned earlier that your team and others across the administration are doing outreach to folks on the wait list to connect them to 2K seats and after school programs.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Have you quantified how many families have been successfully connected from the wait list to other child care options?

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: No, we don't have a way to quantify that, but I can say that there are about 6,000 children that are enrolled in CCBG-funded New York City public school seats.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: There may be children in other...

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: New York City public school seats, meaning funded by other funding streams, and we don't have currently the ability to do a data match with DYCD.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So without a data match, we know that there's tens of thousands of children who are in desperate need of support.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And while we know this is a major commitment for Governor Hochul and Mayor Mamdani,

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We're in the lurch.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: These families are left in the lurch.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So just to understand, it certainly seems that there is, with the historic investment in the state budget, there are resources available for this fiscal year to pay for additional vouchers.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Without concern about future years, can you confirm that's accurate?

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: As you know, the budget is either being signed or signed potentially while we're sitting here.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But the CHOC year funding was determined five months ago.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We all know what those numbers are, and we've known them for five months.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The mayor and the governor did an announcement on like the fifth day in January, and the numbers have not moved.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So just to affirm a simple question, for this fiscal year coming up, FY27, do we have funding for additional vouchers?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Not for future years.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I understand there's a major liability there.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: Well, I do want to say that I haven't seen the, I haven't had a chance to look at the final bills.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: So I'll take your word for it, but the language is complex and we do need to look at that.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: And we would be remiss if we didn't to ensure that it is exactly the same as the executive budget.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: However, we are always looking at where we are on spending and enrollments across the three agencies that use this funding, HRA, ACS, as you know, and New York City Public Schools.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: And so now that the state budget is final, we will need to look at and analyze how and

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: when we will be able to move the wait list.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: I can't confirm today what that time window looks like.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
