utterances.text: So where I'm going with this, and if chairs who I adore, who I didn't say anything nice about you at the beginning of my comments, I apologize.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I do.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Two quick ones.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I do adore them.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: That was very sincere.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I say that to all the chairs, but I really mean it today.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So just to ask, I understand that we have an enormous responsibility.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We significantly increased enrollment for these non-mandated low-income child care vouchers during COVID with an expectation of federal funds lasting forever, and we're not there.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And we've been successful for two years in a row with tremendous advocacy in Albany to maintain that every family who has a voucher is able to keep it.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: That's an enormous achievement.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: You guys deserve credit.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Everybody involved deserves credit.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: God bless.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But we do have a 25,000-person waiting list.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The suggestion I want to make to you on the record here is that we do SCCF vouchers again.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: This was the model we crafted during the de Blasio administration.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: It can be funded for a single year.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We don't make an in perpetuity commitment that this child is going to have a voucher until they're 13 and age out, but that we add child care capacity for this coming fiscal year with the resources we know we have today.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And I think that is the approach we should take in this moment to help reduce the waiting list and help more families connect to care now.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And if we can get more money from Albany in the future, then let's enroll people in the vouchers in the future.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But let's do more today.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Last question.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: If you want to respond to that, you're welcome.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Anything you want to add?

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: We note your recommendation.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I'm not shy.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Last thing, I am deeply disturbed by one policy that didn't make it into the state budget, which has to do with OCFS's policy around

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: hours that parents work, and access to child care vouchers.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Basically, OCFS has regulations that require parents to, that only allow for a child to have a voucher during the precise hours that a parent is working.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So if a parent works nights, then they don't get a daytime child care voucher.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: If a parent works Mondays and Tuesdays, then the kid can only go into the child care center Mondays and Tuesdays.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: If this policy is going to be imposed on the city, it would be profoundly disruptive.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: It would lead to child center closures because how can a child care center that has 100 kids have a different policy of what hours every 100 kid is coming in?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: You can't hire teachers that way.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We will see enormous, enormous disruptions to our child care system.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I am hopeful that the governor is able to guide her agency to do the right thing and that OCFS no longer...

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And OCFS sees that this would be a disastrous policy for New York City child care.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Do you have any updates on your conversations on this?

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Is OCFS giving any indication to you that they recognize how harmful and disruptive a policy this would be?

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: I mean, first I'll say, you know, but for the good of all, you know that ACS has been advocating

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: Yes, no, no, no.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: I'm not criticizing you.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: No, I just want to understand the position of the agency and the position of this administration, which is that we agree that this would be bad for providers, bad for families, bad for child development.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: We have been advocating at the OCFS level and having conversations there.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: The administration has been speaking with chamber about this.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: I don't have any updates, but we are holding on to the same hope that you are, that there is a pathway so that we can ensure that families who are juggling more than just their work schedule, as we know, have the opportunity to have the care for the health of their families.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: There's, I think, four or five working days left in session.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Whether we pass the heavy sea brisboard bill again, which is one way to fix this, or OCFS changes its regulations, which is another way to fix this, we just need it fixed because the families in New York City...

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you, Lincoln.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Sorry, I'm done.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Bye.

chapters.summary: Gutierrez uplifts concerns about CAMBA and Forestdale cuts and asks about voucher recertification rates. Volcomore reports 6-7% of families cannot continue due to federal priority rules for unhoused, special needs, and very low-income families. Gutierrez asks about siblings unable to qualify.
chapters.title: Councilmember Gutierrez questions prevention cuts and voucher recertification data

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Gutierrez, followed by Majority Leader, I don't know if he's here or not, but we'll move on if not.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Good to see everyone.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Welcome, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I want to just uplift the remarks of my colleagues, Councilmember Shulman, about the preventative service.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I understand the role of the Chief Savings Officer.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Obviously, I think we're all in agreement.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: We want to be able to...

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Fund programs that we have money for and fund effective programs.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I do want to say that families in my district, I know the folks from CAMBA and Forest Taylor here, they will be testifying.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: But I really think that putting something out or making a proposal that cuts, severely cuts this program

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: without much of like a safety, without an explanation of what's going to happen to those jobs, without explaining what's going to happen to those families impacted is deeply, deeply concerning.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And you heard from Chair Lee about just the very simple math of the cost, the eventual cost savings to the city when we do invest in preventative care.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: So I just want to uplift Councilmember Lee.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I, of course, am also asking some questions about the child care vouchers.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: It's a hot topic.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And mainly because obviously we care deeply about early child care, as I think this administration does.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: But we have families that are saying, what about me?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: When the mayor is talking about child care, he's not talking about me.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: He's not talking about the voucher that I'm desperately waiting on.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: So I just have some data if you have them specifically, and I'll sign up for round two if I need.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: But can you share what percentage, as I understand it, every family was able to recertify within this fiscal year?

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: The vast majority of families have the opportunity to recertify, provided they submit a recertification application, it's timely, it's complete.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: There is a small percentage, it's about, and not to minimize how important child care is to this population, but there are about 6% to 7% of families that we serve that are not able to continue with their voucher because when we have limited funding, we are required by federal rules

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: to serve three populations first, families that are unhoused, families with a child with special needs, and families that are very low income, which is under 300% of the state income standard.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: And so that's around 6% of those folks unable to kind of maintain their voucher.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Do you have a sense of those that were able to recertify, how many of them potentially have siblings, younger siblings that were not able to qualify?

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Because I think that puts parents in basically back to square one, where they have one child who is utilizing a voucher, but they technically can't go back to work because they have a younger child.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Do you have a sense of that?

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: I don't have a sense of magnitude.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: We know it's an issue.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: I mean, we do have families reaching out to us telling us that they are trying to add a child to their case, and that child might be on the wait list if the family is still eligible serving other children.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: we can look into whether or not those analytics are possible and get back to you.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Got it.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Just so you know, a few of my colleagues and myself jointly sent a letter to some of our folks at the state, knowing full and well the budget would not have been passed by April 1st.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Obviously, we don't have anything.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: I think what you've shared today is just nothing substantial.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: My last question, and I'll stand up for round two, is are there any...

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: Specific circumstances outside of seeing this miraculously in the state budget that would allow for ACS to begin offering vouchers to eligible families?

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: Well, I want to be clear that we will be looking at what is in the state budget, which is a substantial increase in funding that is allowing us at baseline to continue the policy that we have in place.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: To restart, to have employees to restart, which is not nothing.

utterances.speaker: Jennifer Gutiérrez
utterances.text: It is not nothing.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: And we will be looking at where enrollment stands across all three agencies and spending to assess if there is a time in the next months that we may be able to begin moving the wait list.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: But we don't know that yet because we need to assess what's in the budget, what attrition in our system looks like, what spending across ACS, HRA, and New York City Public Schools looks like.

utterances.speaker: Elizabeth Volcomore
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Williams asks about short and midterm racial equity goals. Jones-Gaston lists short-term goals including school-based prevention and community referrals, and midterm goals including staff training, community spaces, reducing unnecessary SCR reports, and restorative approaches in juvenile justice.
chapters.title: Deputy Speaker Williams questions ACS racial equity plan goals

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, Deputy Speaker Williams, followed by Councilmember Mealy.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Good morning.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: I'm just going to ask a few questions about the racial equity plans.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: I'm asking all the agencies these questions.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: We're super excited to have the prelim plans and want to make sure that as we talk about the budget, that we have a lens of racial equity.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: So what has ACS determined are the short and midterm goals to achieve in relations to the equity plan?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I started to say good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Good morning, Deputy Speaker.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: It feels like afternoon already.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I know I said this for another question, but I just want to say for the record that first Deputy Commissioner's, Wynette Saunders, who's leading this work for us, wanted to be here to be able to answer this question, but is not.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So I'm here answering it for her, but she unfortunately couldn't make it today for a medical appointment.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: We love her.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: It's okay.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So on the short-term goals, we have launching school-based early support prevention.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Increasing community-based referrals to prevention.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And those are the short term.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Do you want me to do the middle term?

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Yeah, just do it.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Middle term is continuing to invest in training ACS staff and providers to provide quality services and to foster a psychologically safe work environment that embraces equity and transparency.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And another one is investing in the development of community spaces that contribute to healthy child development and family strength, such as the FECs.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Another is to continue to decrease unnecessary child welfare reports by promoting a citywide culture shift among mandated reporters so that they're not calling the SCR when there's a child who doesn't have a safety reason.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And another is to strengthen restorative approaches to addressing conflict in juvenile justice settings.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And then do you want me to do the long term?

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: No, the short and midterm is fine.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I'm happy to share the full plan with you.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I think it's public.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Yeah, it's public.

chapters.summary: Williams asks about budgetary actions needed and how ACS identifies at-risk youth. Jones-Gaston believes goals are achievable within current budget. Ginsberg details expansion of ACEs program serving 400 more youth with $7M CTL, and Girls Just Us expanding to Queens and Bronx with 24 additional young women.
chapters.title: Deputy Speaker Williams questions budgetary actions for racial equity and juvenile justice prevention

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: What if any budgetary actions need to be taken to help achieve these goals?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Well, before Deputy Commissioner Gendel jumps in, I just want to also just kind of chime in here.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: First of all, I was super delighted to know that the agency had a racial equity plan.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And I'm looking forward to kind of digging into it and adding some of my perspective and experience.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: To be able to deepen the work that we're doing with identified goals and focus areas, but also look for other opportunities where we can really focus on

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: decreasing the disproportionality and disparity that's experienced across our communities, particularly in the

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: services that we employ.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: And I'll just, to answer your question, I believe that both the short-term and medium-term goals are all able to be accomplished within our current budget framework, but I will verify that with the first deputy commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: In the city's preliminary racial equity plan, ACS states that it plans to support young people in preventing contact with the juvenile justice system by expanding community-based programs that serve youth before they come to the attention of family court.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: How does ACS plan to identify youth who may be at risk of system involvement, and what specific community-based programs

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: or interventions does the agency plan to expand or invest in, and what resources are included, if any, in the exec plan to support this work?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I'm going to turn it over to Deputy Commissioner Ginsburg to talk a bit about ACEs and Just Us and maybe other items.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: Good morning, nice to see you.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: Thank you for your question.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: As you are aware, we engaged in a large expansion of both ACEs through the CASES program and Girls Just Us in the last year and the upcoming years.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: In FY26, that budget, there will be 24 additional young women who will be served via this expansion.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: It's a program that started in Brooklyn and is going to be expanded to Queens and the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: FY27, there is also 0.8 million CTL being added into that budget.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: ACES, which is a program, it's the only ROCA program that exists in the city.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: It's an extremely important program for heavily street-involved young people.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: It's one of the only of its kind in the city.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: We have expanded that program so that 400 more young people will be able to take part in that.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: FY27, $7 million of CTL has been dedicated to that program, and in 28, $7 million also.

chapters.summary: Pletnikoff corrects her earlier statement, noting the actual revenue impact from savings is $14.91 million, not $28.9 million as she mistakenly stated in haste.
chapters.title: Deputy Commissioner Pletnikoff corrects revenue takedown figure for the record

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Before we move on, Councilmember Lee, can we correct something for the record from one of your earlier questions?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: Yeah, I apologize.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: When you'd asked me about the revenue impact, I rushed and I misstated.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: The actual revenue impact is $14.91 million.

utterances.speaker: Margaret Pletnikoff
utterances.text: I'd accidentally said $28.9 in my haste, so I apologize.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great, thank you so much.

chapters.summary: Mealy raises concerns about youth sleeping on the floor at Crossroads and questions the three-bed expansion. Jones-Gaston denies anyone sleeps on the floor. Ginsberg clarifies plans for 28 additional beds at Crossroads with two new halls and classroom space, separate from the 6-to-9-bed non-secure conversion.
chapters.title: Councilmember Mealy questions Crossroads detention facility beds and conditions

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, Councilmember Mealy, followed by Councilmember Brewer.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Yes, congratulations.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs, for this great hearing.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: I want to concur with my colleague, Juan, in regards to juvenile.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: You said most of this funding will support administrative and operation costs.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Including supplies, maintenance, and repairs that are not capital eligible at the Crossroad Detention Center in Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: A portion of these funds, at least a half a million, which is baseline now,

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: in fiscal 27 will support three additional beds in non-secure detention facilities.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: I'm glad to hear that the half a million is going in because I have to now, I put funding in with Ready, Willing, and Able to clean all around that detention center.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: I think you'll have to look into that to see when they're going to be moving the cars and make sure that area is

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Neat.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: I'm making sure that area is.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: And I just was there last year in regards to the last administration.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: We put in, I was going to put in about $5 million this year, and last administration had some from Omni.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: To build a whole new bedding facility there.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: And now I'm hearing you're only doing three beds in a non-secure.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: We had the contractors there.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: We was taking one of the courts, because they have at least about three courts in it, and we said we could take one of the courts to make sure

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: that our young men are not sleeping on the floor, in which I went myself walking through, numerous of our young people are sleeping on the floor.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: And now to hear that y'all put money in, a million, to put it in for some of the classrooms, to 20 to 40 classrooms,

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Isn't this kind of a mix-up?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Yeah, I think we can explain some of it, and then I'll turn it over to Deputy Commissioner Ginsburg.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So the plan you're talking about, so first of all, I want to state, first of all, no child, young person is sleeping on the floor.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: I was there myself.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Some young people have temporary beds, but nobody is sleeping on the floor.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: They have chairs.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: They put the chairs together to sleep in.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: No, no, no.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: No, they are giving out beds.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: We should do a walkthrough, really.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: We'd be happy to.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: do that for sure.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Sometimes it's at major capacity and that's why they don't have enough beds.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: So that's why we put more money in to make sure that no one is.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: And I gotta say Crossroads sometimes have more employees than

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: our young people in there.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: So it fluctuates, but recently it's been almost 90%.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So we are building a temporary structure at Crossroads.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Three beds.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: No, no, no.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So the three beds is different.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So last year's budget included funding, capital funding,

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: either capital expense, Margaret will correct me, to do expense funding to do a somewhat temporary facility at Crossroads to address capacity.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: This year's budget includes funding unrelated to Crossroads and unrelated to secure detention.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: To take a non-secure detention facility and make it a slightly large, serve more children for close to home.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: So completely separate from Crossroads.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: From six to nine beds, but nothing to do with Crossroads.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Crossroads, do you want to talk more?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You guys are on the best.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: So at Crossroads, we are planning to build an additional 28 beds.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: There you go.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: That will be two halls.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: Each hall will have education and programming space.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: We are also planning for additional classroom space to accommodate the increased population.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Okay, 28 beds sound more like it.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: We're with you and we appreciated your visit and you're welcome to come back anytime.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Please think about the maintenance around the facility.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: It's the eyesore on the community because they never move the cars.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: The 73rd precinct is right next.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Cross the street.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: They don't move their cars, so I have to put funds just to make sure that the community look nice.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: We appreciate your partnership on that.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: We are struggling with the fact that we are a 24-7 operation.

utterances.speaker: Nancy Ginsberg
utterances.text: And our staff needs some place to park their cars.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: But sometime we got to take up one side of the month and just ask everyone to move it to the next side of the floor.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: You know, on the street.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Because I see the DYCD mob coming in, so we do have to start wrapping it up.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks about support for children in foster care whose parents are incarcerated at Rikers. Mendez describes the CHIP team facilitating visits and case planning. Brewer requests data on reunification possibilities and additional funding, which Jones-Gaston commits to providing.
chapters.title: Councilmember Brewer questions ACS connection with incarcerated parents at Rikers

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Okay, Councilmember Brewer.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I have a question about Rikers.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I go to Rikers a lot, and I have been a foster parent, and then now I have the migrant kids in my house.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So I'm familiar with the crew.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: My question is, people coming out of Rikers or coming out from upstate, women in particular, are you in touch with the Department of Correction?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Because we all know that maybe the kids are in foster care.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: They really want to go back with their parents.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: They can't go back until they have a place to live, blah, blah, blah.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I worry about that population because that population I know well.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: You get mental health issues when your parents are incarcerated.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So do you have that kind of connection at all, or is that something that you're focused on, either with Fortune Society, Correction, or anybody else?

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: I just want to make sure we're clear.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Are you asking about children in foster care whose parents are incarcerated?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And I'm also concerned.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: It's not a big population.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I don't know, 400 or 500 women at Rikers.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: But it is a population where young people can be damaged mentally if they don't get support.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: That's important to us, and I'll pass it.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So thank you for your question.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: ACS has a team.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: It's called CHIP, Children of Incarcerated Parents.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: And we work very closely with the foster care providers to ensure while children are in care and their parents are incarcerated throughout the state or even in the country to facilitate visits.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: And that team also works closely with agencies because case planning should continue.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: And when parents are released and they're moving back into the area, if the goal remains reunification, there's an expectation that the agencies are supporting those families to

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: assess and make a decision about safe and timely reunification.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: To your question about a formal agreement or partnership, we don't have that now, but we can definitely take that and look at that.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Obviously, the new DOC commissioner is phenomenal, known him for 40 years.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And the fact of the matter is I think he would be interested in it.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: We're trying to reduce the population.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: This would be one way to do it.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: You know the issues.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I don't know if there's funding allocated to it.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I don't know what the numbers are, but I'd love to see some data as to how many women could be reunited.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: You can know where this is going so that the families are in better shape, the women are in better shape, and they get support.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Is that something that you could get back to us on?

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Okay, number two would be...

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: I get in trouble when I say I'll look into that.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So yes, I think so.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: It's a really important population and they're going to end up in recidivism if that issue...

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: It's not addressed, in my opinion.

utterances.speaker: Rebecca Jones Gaston
utterances.text: Well, and I'll remove her getting in trouble and say yes, we'll absolutely follow up, and I look forward to meeting the commissioner.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks about numbers of youth aging out and support to prevent homelessness. Mendez reports 420 non-permanency discharges in 2024 and 383 in 2025, with Fair Futures, CityFHEPS vouchers, Anthos aftercare, and Supervision to 21 team providing support.
chapters.title: Councilmember Brewer questions foster care transition and aging-out youth support

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Second issue quickly is between Fair Fair, a wonderful program, and the fact that True Colors and others are trying to deal with young people coming out of foster care, becoming adults, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So what are the numbers on that?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: In other words, because we don't want people to end up in shelters.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: We want people to have a life after foster care.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: True Colors helps, others help.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I just want to know what the numbers are and what the, it says here in this question that there's money allocated toward coming out of foster care, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So what are the numbers and how are we doing on that topic?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Because there are people, unfortunately, in shelters who I don't think should be there.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Agreed.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So for calendar year 2024, we had 420 young people non-permanency discharges.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: That number is down in calendar year 2025 to 383.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: We work very closely with the foster care providers who do a wonderful job engaging young people and preparing them for transition.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So there are independent living skills that are provided prior.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: We have our fair futures.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: We work very closely to understand and assess the child's needs post.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Foster care?

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Will they need supportive housing?

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: We have, we work, you know, with our supportive, with HRA and HPD to make sure that we, if they need supportive housing, they can go there.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: We work closely with Anthos, and we have CityFEP's vouchers, so young people get CityFEP's vouchers.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Laura Lazarus, yes, keep going.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: And Anthos does aftercare, so they help with the stabilization after, because we know that once you just get your keys, there's more to maintain.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I'm aware, I'm very aware.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: Fair Futures, I think you know, is up to 26.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: For those that decide to leave care prior to turning 21, we have a team called Supervision to 21 that does their best to track these young people, providing them supports, connecting them to services.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: And then if need be, we'll hold a conference to see if they need to come back.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So I say all that to say we do try as much as possible to have a net around these individuals to support them.

utterances.speaker: Ina Mendez
utterances.text: So the organization that you mentioned will make sure that our providers are aware to connect the young people to that organization and also the Center for Fair Futures.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: futures as they're working with the coaches.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: I guess my question would be not right now, but is there extra funding or are there extra resources despite all the amazing work that you, Fair Futures, Laura, and everybody else is doing?

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: That would be my question.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: You always need more money, but in this case, this is a really, again, a really important group.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: I can take that back and look into that.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: I would appreciate it.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: I don't know.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: This population is recidivism if you don't address it now.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: And we're really, that's why the partnership with Anthos is so critical.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: Because they provide that year of stabilization.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: And so we're hoping that that will continue.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: I know Laura very well.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: So 383 is what?

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: What is that number?

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: I'm sorry.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: So that is the number of youth non-permanency discharges.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: Not in.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: If I'm correct, those are young people that left care not to adoption, reunification, or key care.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Ones who need help.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilman Maru.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks if providers being cut have been notified in writing. Jones-Gaston says not yet in writing but leadership was contacted verbally. Stevens then asks about CPS vacancies; officials report 191 vacancies in Division of Child Protection with 108 CPS positions vacant.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens lightning round on prevention contract notifications and CPS vacancies

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I'm going to do a lightning round just because I know that there's a couple of folks who still want to ask a few questions.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I guess my first question is just around the preventative service of contract.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Have we notified that are being cut?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Have we notified those providers in writing yet?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And have they received the date of when those contracts will be ending?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And if not, when will that be going out?

utterances.speaker: ACS Commissioner
utterances.text: So, and I can be corrected, we have not notified in writing yet.

utterances.speaker: ACS Commissioner
utterances.text: I did have an opportunity to touch base with each of the organization's leadership yesterday.

utterances.speaker: ACS Commissioner
utterances.text: And we are waiting for kind of final approval on the budget, and then we'll move forward with written communication and then the timeline of the 90 days as stated earlier.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Okay, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: The executive plan includes a budget headcount of 385 for protective services for areas.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: How many vacancies are there currently in this program?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I'm going to do a couple of questions.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Were any vacancies in this program area eliminated as part of the mayor's savings?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: If so, how many and why?

utterances.speaker: ACS Commissioner
utterances.text: I'm going to turn that over to Deputy Commissioner Linares.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Linares
utterances.text: Hi again.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Linares
utterances.text: So we have the contract capacity for family support model again is 4,512.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Linares
utterances.text: Know that that's about 44% of our total capacity, which is 10,637.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Linares
utterances.text: In April, again, we had a total of 3,347 families that were participating in these services.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Linares
utterances.text: So we have over 1,000 contracted slot capacity to serve families in the family support programs.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Linares
utterances.text: That doesn't account for the other programs that we have available in the boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Linares
utterances.text: We do not anticipate that any of the families will be waitlisted.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Linares
utterances.text: And we want to talk, there's the different reasons why there are some vacancies.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Linares
utterances.text: So we've taken steps to increase utilization.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Linares
utterances.text: We have worked closely with DHS and NYCHA to make sure that we are in the community.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Linares
utterances.text: We do things like Family Day and invite prevention providers to the table so that they can connect with families in an effort to make sure that their utilization is increased.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Linares
utterances.text: Hence, I think we have, you can see an increase in our community.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Referrals, where we've gone up to about, from last year, the same time, about 20%.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: Were you also asking, sorry, were you also asking about child protective?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Sorry, I misunderstood.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: So the plan doesn't have any new savings there.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: We currently have 191 vacancies in the Division of Child Protection.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: Our CPS, the actual Child Protective Specialist, budget position is 1,855, and there are 108 that are vacant.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: 108 that are vacant.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks about background check timelines and onboarding for child protective specialists. Officials report about six weeks for background checks, several months of training at Satterwhite Academy including simulation, and approximately 70% of candidates complete the process.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens questions CPS hiring timeline and training completion rates

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: What is the typical timeline for frontline staff, such as child protective specialists?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Does ACS perform its own background checks on candidates?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: If so, how long does it take?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And if not, how does those background checks take place?

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: There is a background check policy.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: It varies by position what's required, and so it would vary how long it takes.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Do you have like an average?

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: It's about six weeks.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Six weeks.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: The expert.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: How does the onboarding typically take place and train a child protective specialist?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And what is the percentage of CPS candidates provided job offers actually complete the hiring process and onboarding?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: What difficulties has ACS faced in hiring those workers?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And what is ACS doing to address those issues?

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: So we have a very extensive training program for new CPS.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: They spend several hours.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: months at the Satterwhite Academy and that also includes we have a simulation site where part of the job is practicing what it's like to go to court what it's like to go to home

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: and then they go into a training unit in their offices where they have a small caseload and less complicated cases and then they go back to training in the academy

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: because they'll have had a better sense of what the job's like when they've done a few cases and they go back to the academy.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: So it takes several months, and I think about 75, like about three quarters complete.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: Is that right?

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: Closer to 70%, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Those are all the questions I have now.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I still have a number of questions.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: We'll send them to you in writing because I know we are kind of pressed for time.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so just want to just remind our colleagues and you guys to try to be as succinct as possible because DYCD is in the other room waiting, please.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Lee announces round two questions, with Councilmember Joseph followed by Councilmember Schulman.
chapters.title: Chair Lee begins round two of ACS questioning

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, round two.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We got Councilmember Joseph followed by Councilmember Shulman.

chapters.summary: Joseph asks for current numbers at Crossroads and Horizon. Officials confirm 181 at Horizon and 186 at Crossroads, with capacity of 245 bedrooms each. Joseph notes the facilities are near capacity.
chapters.title: Councilmember Joseph questions detention facility capacity numbers

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Just wanted to ask a quick question.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: How many young people we have at Crossroads and how many at Horizon?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Real quick.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: 181 at Horizon, 186 at Crossroads.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: What is the capacity the facilities were built for?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: All right, so.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: Bedrooms.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Okay, we're gonna hold that right there.

chapters.summary: Joseph asks about ACS working with NYC Public Schools on transportation for foster care students. Mendez details the reimbursement plan established in 2022, recently revised with increased daily rates and expanded eligibility, with ACS providing transportation for Children's Center youth.
chapters.title: Councilmember Joseph questions foster care student transportation to school

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: The city has a unique obligation for students in foster care who are under the city's care and custody and must do more to support their education, including ensuring that they get to school in the first place.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: These are feasible options.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Has ACS worked with New York City Public Schools to identify and implement a solution that allows students to get to school as soon as they are placed in foster care or change foster care homes as utilizing ACS vehicles?

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: So, yes.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: So in 2022, in October, November, we worked with New York City Public Schools, the foster care office and the transportation office, and we released a reimbursement plan and guidelines around transportation.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: And it outlined that as soon as children come into care, the school should be notified and then providers will be reimbursed.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: At the time, it was up to $200 a day if they had to cover the transportation because the providers are the foster parent.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: And we've had that plan in place.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: We made some revisions, I believe, last school year.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: And then New York City Public Schools, after some additional conversations and hearing from the community, increased the $200 a day.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: So that is a new facet to where in the positive we're revising the transition plan and also have kind of extended who could apply.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: So it's children that are going to school in the city or out of the city.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: And then I believe those that live out of the city that need to come into the city.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: And then also they will reimburse providers if they use other entities that have, there's other types of car service, which wasn't part of the original plan.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: So we're in the process of revising the current policy.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: And so hopefully we'll have that online for the start of the next school year.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: For children at the Children's Center, when you think about it, they are in ACS's custody.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: So we are now the responsible for getting them to, and all of the children are in our custody, but like.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: They're in our house, if you will.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: So we are responsible.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: We have an added interest to make sure they go to school.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: So we use our ACS transportation, and our deputy commissioner of administration coordinates with the Children's Center team to make sure those cars are available, and they have a plan, and they get the children to school.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: And the ones that are in agency service this fall, you will have a plan to make sure they get to and from school because they have the highest absenteeism rate entering into care.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: So we're hoping that, and we'll reiterate with the foster care providers, we're hoping that with this revised policy, they're going to get reimbursed for more money per day.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: they'll be able to use other services like Kids Car, that should increase the attendance and reduce the challenges that they've experienced.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Schulman asks about tools for public school staff to match students with prevention services. Deputy Commissioner Linares explains social service laws require consent forms, so no aggregate data sharing exists, but individual information can be shared with consent. Schulman also requests a briefing on cuts' impact on Forestdale.
chapters.title: Councilmember Schulman questions data sharing between schools and prevention services

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, Councilmember Shulman, followed by Councilmember Gutierrez.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: I have two questions.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: One, are there currently any tools and data sharing agreements in place that make it easier for public school staff to understand student needs and support matching with prevention services?

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Linares
utterances.text: Hi, thank you for that question.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Linares
utterances.text: So as you may know, because of social service laws, we will need,

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Linares
utterances.text: for prevention services, we need consent forms to get information about the education of the children.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Linares
utterances.text: So we don't have a data agreement where we can exchange data aggregately.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Linares
utterances.text: However, if a provider is in need of specific information about a specific child, as long as they have a consent form, we are able to work with them and get them the information that they need.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Linares
utterances.text: But unfortunately, because of service laws, we need a consent.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Linares
utterances.text: So there's been some conversations in turn to try to figure out how to do that.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Commissioner Linares
utterances.text: But we will continue this conversation and try to figure out how to do it in a way that obviously keep the confidentiality of the family safe.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Now, keep us informed.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: And the other is that should these cuts go through for the preventive services, I would like a briefing on how that's going to affect particularly the...

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: The agency in my district.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: That's a yes?

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Yeah, of course.

utterances.speaker: Lynn C. Schulman
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

chapters.summary: Williams asks whether Fair Futures expansion pledged in the racial equity plan will be affected by funding reductions. Officials confirm the program still expands to 5,400 by FY29, with the budget action slowing growth but achieving the same end number.
chapters.title: Deputy Speaker Williams questions Fair Futures expansion and racial equity pledge

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Okay, Deputy Speaker?

utterances.speaker: Deputy Speaker
utterances.text: Yes, I'm back.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Speaker
utterances.text: So back to the racial equity plans.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Speaker
utterances.text: So ACS pledged to expand fair futures to provide justice-involved youth with coaches, housing specialists, and educational services.

utterances.speaker: Deputy Speaker
utterances.text: Given the reduction in funding in fiscal 2026 through 2028 for fair futures that was included as a savings in the exec plan, does ACS still intend to achieve this expansion?

utterances.speaker: Deputy Speaker
utterances.text: Will any slots that were earmarked for the pledges in the racial equity plan be eliminated due to this reduction in funding?

utterances.speaker: Deputy Speaker
utterances.text: And how many additional youth does ACS hope to serve if it does expand the program?

utterances.speaker: Deputy Speaker
utterances.text: When can we expect to see this expansion and how will it be funded?

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: We are continuing to expand Fair Futures, and it is in both on the juvenile justice side and on child welfare.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: The budget action slows the growth, but the growth by fiscal 2029, we achieved the same end number of 5,400, which was the goal.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: When we get to fiscal 2029, if we feel that's not sufficient, obviously we can have conversations about where to go from there.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

chapters.summary: Joseph asks for five-year data on youth aging out and college enrollment. Mendez provides 420 discharges in 2024 and 383 in 2025, with College Choice growing from 200 at launch to 471 in spring 2026. Joseph also asks about re-engagement supports for youth who leave college.
chapters.title: Councilmember Joseph questions foster youth aging out and college enrollment data

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And back to Councilmember Joseph.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Real quick, two more questions.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Over the past five years, how many youth have aged out of foster care annually, and what percentage of those youth are enrolled in college?

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: So I don't have the past five year.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: I can give you,

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: We had in calendar year 2024, 420 non-permanency discharges.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: So those are youth leaving foster care.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: And in calendar 2025, we had 383.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: So I'll have to get the other information and then also crosswalk that with our college choice data.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: We do know that when college choice launched in 2022, we had 200 young people.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: And I believe the spring semester of 2026, we had 471 youth in college.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: But I will have to crosswalk.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: The exits with who was in college at the time.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: No problem.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: For foster youth with foster care, for students with foster care experience who did not complete college, what academic career re-engagement supports are available for them for re-enroll or transition into employment pathways?

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: So for young people that are in college specifically, we work very closely with New York Foundling.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: They do our college support services program, and they provide, in addition to supporting around the financial issues, the wraparound academic.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: advisement.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: So for those that are going to be struggling, let's say they're on probation, they're providing additional support.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: If they do have to leave school, they still, if they're in care, still have their case planning team, they'll have their fair futures coach, and then we have a team at ACS.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: So we do work to re-engage.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: There have been young people that have been in the college choice program, discontinued, and now are looking at our career choice program, which launched this fiscal year.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: So maybe they're going to take a different path.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Cheers.

chapters.summary: Lee thanks the Commissioner and team, and announces a quick break before the DYCD hearing. Stevens asks if the Commissioner survived the questioning.
chapters.title: Chair Lee concludes ACS hearing and announces break

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, I think we are good.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So thank you so much for joining us.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We're just going to take a quick break.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Wait, how was it?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Were we nice?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: You okay?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Did you survive?

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: It was great.

utterances.speaker: Christopher Marte
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: I'll be back.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: I'll be back.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'm just coming back.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'm okay.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Oh, Pret?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: If you go to Pret, let me know.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Just a reminder, everyone, please do not approach the dais.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Please do not approach the dais at any point.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: No food or beverages are allowed.

utterances.speaker: ACS Official
utterances.text: in this room.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Please quiet down.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Please find your seats.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: We are getting ready to begin.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Please find your seats.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Just a reminder, no one may approach the dais at any time, and no food or beverages are allowed in this room.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sorry.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I have my snacks.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Are you guys ready on your end?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: You guys are good?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Waiting for, okay.

chapters.summary: Lee reconvenes for the second hearing on DYCD's FY27 executive budget of $1.62 billion, representing 1.3% of the city budget. She notes a $14.2M decrease from the preliminary plan due to savings from consulting contract elimination, vacancy reduction, and COLA adjustments.
chapters.title: Chair Lee opens DYCD FY27 executive budget hearing

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, I think we can go ahead and get started.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so good afternoon and welcome to today's second hearing about the FY27 executive budget.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I'm Councilmember Linda Lee, and today we will hear from Department of Youth and Community Development.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I'm pleased to be joined again by my colleague, Councilmember and Chair Althea Stevens.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And we have been joined by the Deputy Speaker.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Maloney, Councilmember Epstein, Councilmember Wong, and Councilmember Felder.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And welcome, Commissioner, and your team, and thank you so much for joining us today to answer our questions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I just want to note, it's really cool that the three of us have all this former nonprofit experience, and I think there need to be more of us in government spaces, so I'll just say that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I will just say that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So on May 12, 2026, the administration released the executive financial plan for FY26 through 2030 with a proposed fiscal 27 budget of $124.7 billion.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The Department of Youth and Community Development's proposed FY27 budget of $1.62 billion represents 1.3% of the administration's proposed fiscal 27 budget in the executive plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: This is a decrease of $14.2 million, or less than 1% from the $1.63 billion originally budgeted in the FY27 preliminary plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: This decrease results from several actions, including savings the agency found through elimination of certain consulting contracts, vacancy reduction, COLA adjustments, and underspending in community resources for employment and development, and the ONS programs.

chapters.summary: Lee notes the Council called for $14.2M for runaway and homeless youth programs, peer navigators, and housing navigators. She introduces Commissioner Sandra Escamilla-Davies and notes the three chairs' shared nonprofit experience.
chapters.title: Chair Lee highlights Council priorities for DYCD including runaway homeless youth funding

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: This was slightly offset by addition of funding to expand services for the Crisis Management System program and the supporting wraparound services.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: As of April 26, DYCD's actual headcount of 626 is 71 less than their fiscal 26 budgeted headcount of 690.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In the Council's preliminary budget response, we called on the Mayor to add $14.2 million to DYCD's budget to invest in expense funding

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: for programs related to runaway and homeless youth, peer navigators, and runaway homeless youth housing navigators.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Although these requests were not included in the plan, we remain hopeful that today's hearing will give us the opportunity to discuss a path forward to secure funding for these programs.

chapters.summary: Stevens details DYCD's $1.6B FY27 budget, $14.8M for CMS expansion, $8.8M for Advance and Earn, and $3M savings from 27 vacant positions. She raises concerns about RHY funding not being baselined and praises the new Compass RFP release after 10 years.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens delivers opening statement on DYCD budget and Compass RFP

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And now I want to turn it over to my co-chair for the hearing, Chair Stevens.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Good afternoon and welcome to our second half of the fiscal 2027 executive budget hearing for the Department of Youth and Community Development.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I am Councilman Rautia Stevens, Chair of the Committee on Children and Youth.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Today we will be hearing from DYCD, our new Commissioner, Sandra Escudiz, along with our first deputy.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And our financial officers.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you for joining us today.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: We're here to discuss the agency's recent release of executive plan, which includes a budget of $1.6 billion for fiscal 2027.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: This funding includes $66.2 million in personnel services to support and a budget headcount of 704 positions and a total of $1.55 billion other than personnel.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: The current fiscal 2027 budget is $105.3 million, or 7% more than the fiscal 26th adopted budget.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: However, DYCD's fiscal 2027 budget decreased by $14.2 million from the $1.63 billion budgeted in the fiscal 2027 preliminary financial plan.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: To note a few changes in the executive plan, there's an additional $14.8 million to expand services for the crisis management, CMS programs, and supporting wraparound services in fiscal 2027.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: In fiscal 2028, in the outer years, the plan includes a baseline funding of $19.6 million in the Office of Community Safety.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So for this is a...

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: support the programs in the new office.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: A plan also added $8.8 million for the Office of Economic Opportunity Adjustments for the Advance and Earn program that helps train and employ young people ages 16 through 24.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: The plan also includes the savings agencies found to meet the targets set by the Office of Management and Budget.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: That included $6.3 million for over-budgeting in the Community Resources for Employment and Development, CRED, and ONS programs.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: A surplus cost of...

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Cost of living adjustment resulting in a 31 million after assessing that providers distribution funding needed to provide the 3% increase for human service contract.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I want to ensure that the COLA adjustment does not impact programming and provides adequate funding to the providers.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I'm concerned that the executive plan

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: includes baseline city fund savings of $3 million from the elimination of 27 vacant positions beginning in fiscal 2026.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Again, I want to ensure that actions don't impact programming for youth.

chapters.summary: Stevens acknowledges concerns from providers losing Compass contracts and urges DYCD to evaluate the RFP process for future procurements. She pushes for completing Beacons and Cornerstones RFPs while the new commissioner with provider experience is in place.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens addresses Compass RFP provider concerns and calls for more RFPs

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: The executive budget did not include fiscal 2026 one-shot funding for RHY funding and peer

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: navigators, which is something that I've been fighting consistently with to make sure that we're funding because we do not want to forget about our young people.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I do hope the administration will provide baseline funding and the adopted budget for the navigators because that was a program that we lost and then we got the council brought back.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so we want to make sure that this is something that's critical because the young people and providers have said it's a critical.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Service need.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Our youth better relate to their peers.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: This gives them a sense of belonging, a sense of understanding.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: The housing navigators assess in navigating the young people through transition and a safe, dependable housing.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: The services for RHY youth does not stop at the door, but it begins at the door.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
