utterances.text: I'm pleased that after 10 years,

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: It was a long time.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: A new Compass RFP for elementary and middle school students was released, and I do appreciate the dedication and hard work by DYCD.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I really want to give it up to you guys because everyone knows that I was so critical that they

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: put this on you guys in the turnaround.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So it was done.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I knew it was gonna get done.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: No one, I mean, Dan, you are a little grayer, I'm not gonna lie, but you still look good.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: They aged you, I see it.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: But you guys worked really hard to get it done and work it through, and I'm really happy.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: However, you know, with new RFPs, there are always these mitigating factors that come in.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: including myself and everyone here and and councilman Williams just came to me and said she's also being spammed about the campus RFP we are getting so many calls about

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: a lot of worried providers who have to fold as a result of the outcome of the deficit of services they will be able to provide to the communities and

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I'm concerned and I know as our new commissioners come in she is being so thoughtful and having a perspective of a former provider of trying to figure out the best way forward and make sure that

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: everyone is whole including the providers.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: You know I think sometimes we talk about it as if the providers are not a workforce as well and you know this council has spent a lot of time and energy talking about the workforce but for some reason we forget to keep fighting for human service workers

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: and I think that is just as important and so when we do these contracts we have to and I know you guys have been thoughtful I think as a council we have to think about like how do we protect them as well and that's something that I'm dedicated and have been dedicated to it

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: there are other DYCD programs that are overdue for the RFP like my new fight the beacons and cornerstones

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I want the agency to take this opportunity to further evaluate the compass RFP examine every process every methodology and matrix used to identify changes that can be made for the next RFP

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: so that it has a fair and more an equitable process and honestly I

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I think that if any, we probably need to get all the RFPs done while Sandra's here.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I'm going to just say it.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Because I just have a feeling that with that provider background, it'll look a lot different than this one did.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So I'm going to be pushing for a lot more RFPs, just so we're clear, so we can have a nice...

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Stable programs so that when I'm gone, we'll be in good hands.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so I'm excited about that.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so I'll be pushing for that.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Everyone here today in fiscal 2027 executive budget hearing has a responsibility with the objective of providing the best services to the youth of the city.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: It is my expectation from DYCD.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: You guys will be responsive to questions and concerns of the council members.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: It is my hope that fiscal 2027 adopt the budget meets the goals set forth by council.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: We want to ensure that every youth and adult program provides the essentials, necessities, including adequate funding to meet the everyday needs.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I would like to recognize, I've recognized most of my staff already, but I want to recognize the financial, the fiscal staff, Sandra Gray, our senior fiscal analyst, Aaliyah Aaliyah, assistant director.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And I don't see Isha on here, but also Isha, shout out to her because I've been driving her crazy as well.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So with that, I will turn it over to Chair Lee.

chapters.summary: Lee reminds attendees that public testimony is June 10th at 9:30 AM, recognizes additional council members, and committee counsel swears in Commissioner Escamilla-Davies and her team.
chapters.title: Chair Lee reminds public of June 10 testimony date and swears in DYCD witnesses

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And just as a reminder, really quickly, we're having all of our public testimony for all the agencies on Wednesday, June 10th, beginning at 9.30 a.m.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So Wednesday, June 10th, beginning at 9.30 a.m.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: is when we're having a long all day.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We're going to have so much fun all day of public testimony hearings, so please make sure to sign up for those.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, and then without, oh wait, hold on.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We've also been joined by Councilmember Jay Sanchez.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Everyone's going to start thinking your first name, his first name is Jay, Justin Sanchez, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Ressler, Councilmember Wilson, welcome our newest councilmember, and Councilmember Joseph.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And with that, I'll turn it over to Committee Counsel to swear in the witnesses for their testimony.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good afternoon.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Do you affirm to tell the truth, the whole truth, and nothing but the truth before this committee and to respond honestly to council member questions?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Commissioner Ascamilla-Davis?

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Deputy Commissioner Haskell, Deputy Commissioner Mulligan, Chief Financial Officer Bailey,

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Chief Accountability Officer Catalini, and Associate Commissioner Guillen.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: You may begin.

chapters.summary: Escamilla-Davies shares her three-month listening tour findings, noting DYCD's growth in scale and complexity. She outlines her team's examination of procurement, contracting, payments, and performance management to ensure responsive, effective operations.
chapters.title: Commissioner Escamilla-Davies introduces herself and DYCD's organizational assessment

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: Good afternoon, Chair Stevens and Lee and members of the Finance and Children and Youth Committee.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: I am Commissioner Sandra Escamilla-Davies and I am joined by Chief Financial Officer Navita Bailey and other DYC staff who are here and available to answer your questions today.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: Thank you for the opportunity to discuss DYCD's fiscal year 2027 executive budget.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: Since becoming commissioner, I have spent the past few, three months, listening and learning from staff, providers, and partners across the city.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: What has become clear is that DYCD is powered by extraordinary dedicated workforce and a provider network that continues to deliver critical services for children, youth, and families every day.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: As the agency has grown significantly over the years, both in scale and complexity, my team and I are taking a thoughtful look at how our internal infrastructures, staffing, operational systems support that work.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: This includes examining areas such as procurement, contracting, payments, program operations, and performance management so that DYCD can continue to operate in a way that is responsive, effective, and aligned with the evolving needs of New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: At the same time, we are engaging providers and community partners to better understand both operational challenges and opportunities for innovation.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: across our programs and services.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: These conversations are helpful to inform how we strengthen and broaden the ecosystem that supports young people and families across this great city.

chapters.summary: Escamilla-Davies presents the $1.619B budget, with $1.5B in tax levy, $105.4M federal, $18.4M state, and $5.2M in-city funds. She details $10.9M in FY26 savings and $34.8M in FY27, from in-house audits and cost estimate readjustments, with no service cuts.
chapters.title: Commissioner presents DYCD FY27 budget overview and savings

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: The executive budget presented today reflects Mayor Mamdani's vision for a more affordable, equitable, and safer New York City and demonstrates a continued commitment to the children, youth, families, and communities that DYCD serves every day.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: Despite broader fiscal pressures facing the city, the budget protects DYCD's core programming and continues to invest in services and opportunities that support young people and strengthen communities across all five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: DYCD proposed a series of savings totaling $10.9 million in the current fiscal year, $34.8 million next year, and $32.7 million in the outer years.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: This includes savings from conducting audit functions in-house, along with savings from readjustments to cost estimates.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: The budget stands at $1.619 billion compared to $1.633 billion in fiscal 2027 preliminary budget.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And the breakdown is as follows.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: $1.5 billion in tax levy funds, $105.4 million in federal funds, $18.4 million or about

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: 1.13% in state funding, and $5.2 million in interest city funds.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: These are

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: Resources support a wide range of investments across the agency's portfolio.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: While not an exhaustive list, I want to highlight several key initiatives that reflect how these funds strengthen services for young people, families, and communities across the city.

chapters.summary: Escamilla-Davies details the Compass RFP with $727M in FY27 funding, 121 center-based awards with 7,840 seats, 806 school-based awards with 95,841 seats, 8,621 additional seats, and 52 new community-based organizations. She acknowledges providers who lost contracts.
chapters.title: Commissioner highlights Compass RFP as largest after-school re-procurement in a decade

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: First, I'll start with the Compass request for proposals.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: Compass is the nation's largest after-school system serving elementary and middle school students across New York City through Compass Elementary and schools out New York City.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: The release of the Compass Center-Based and School-Based Awards marks the very first major re-procurement and modernization of after-school contracts in more than a decade, and that is just so exciting.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: This investment reflects what families, schools, and providers have consistently called for.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: Expanded access, stronger program, quality, workforce stability, and greater equity across the after-school system.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: For fiscal year 2027, the city committed to $727 million in Compass funding.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: This investment supports the additional 20,000 new elementary school seats over three school years and increased the price per participant rate to better support provider stability, staffing, and high-quality program for children and families.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: DYCD announced, and I want to highlight some of the things from this RFP, 121 center-based awards representing 7,840 seats.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: six school-based awards representing 95,841 seats, expansions into 71 new elementary schools across 32 high-need community districts,

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: 8,621 additional citywide seats, and 52 new community-based organizations joining the Compass portfolio, including at least one in every borough.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: This RFP also introduced an updated program model designed to better reflect the evolving needs of young people and families, including strong expectations around social-emotional learning,

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: career exploration, and future readiness, expanded inclusion supports, enhanced program quality standards, and greater alignment between the after-school programming and broader youth development goals.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: The increased investment in Compass represents an investment in stability for the sector by supporting stronger staffing structures, better wages, more sustainable programs,

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: models, and expanding access for children and families across New York City.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: We are proud of this expanded access, strengthening program models, and new investments reflected in these awards.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: It really is noteworthy.

chapters.summary: Escamilla-Davies reports 150,000 Summer Rising applications, 127,000 offers extended (3,500 more than last year), 300 sites including 19 additional buildings, and enhanced collaboration with NYC Public Schools for students with disabilities and enrichment.
chapters.title: Commissioner details Summer Rising 2026 plans and expanded access

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: Yet at the same time, I want to recognize that there were providers who were not awarded contracts through this process.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: For some organizations, this transition may have significant financial and operational impacts, particularly for providers that have served children.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: families and school communities for many years.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: We do not take that lightly.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: I do not take that lightly.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: Those contributions, relationships, and years of service are meaningful and worthy of recognition and respect.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And yet it is also true that this was a competitive procurement process guided by clearly defined evaluation criteria, including program quality, organizational capacity, and operational readiness.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: In partnership with New York City Public Schools, the process also incorporated principal input for school-based programs.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: As implementation moves forward, we are committed to focus on supporting thoughtful transitions, maintaining continuity for young people and families, and working closely with schools,

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: outgoing providers, incoming providers, and communities to help open strong in the fall.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: The second program that I want to highlight is Summer Rising.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: The Summer Rising 2026 application portal opened on March 3rd and closed on March 27th, during which time more than 150,000 applications were submitted by families across New York City.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: Using a data-driven understanding of enrollment, patterns, and demand, the city extended approximately 127,000 offers to families, and this is an increase of 3,500 offers compared to last summer.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: Available seats will continue to be offered to families throughout the summer.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: To support expanded access, New York City Public Schools opened 19 additional buildings, bringing the total of summer rising sites citywide to 300.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: DYCD and New York City Public Schools continue to work closely together to strengthen and expand high-quality summer enrichment opportunities for young people.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: This includes deeper collaboration between principals and community-based organization, strengthening supports for students with disability, and enhancing program opportunities such as field trips, enhanced activities, and career exploration.

chapters.summary: Escamilla-Davies shares the early mayoral commitment to continue housing navigation, peer navigators, and financial coaches for RHY in FY27. She reports SYEP received a record 230,000 applications, funded at $246M to serve 100,000+ youth, with expanded career-ready programs.
chapters.title: Commissioner highlights Runaway Homeless Youth and SYEP programs

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: The next program I'd like to highlight is Runaway Homeless Youth.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: We are so thankful for the early mayoral commitment to continue the critical role of housing navigation, peer navigators, and financial coaches as part of the services offered youth in our RHY drop-in center and FY27.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: The budget process is ongoing, but we are pleased that the city recognizes the importance of these supports for runaway homeless youth, and providers have the opportunity now to better plan the delivery of service for the upcoming fiscal year.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: We're in the thick of summer youth employment.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: Mayor Mondani officially launched the 2026 summer youth employment on January 20th, marking the earliest application opening in program's history.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: This program received a record 230,000 applications from young people across New York City.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: Funded at $246 million, SYP will once again serve more than 100,000 youth this summer through paid work experiences, career exploration, skill building, and supportive opportunities.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: As one of the nation's largest youth employment programs, SYP

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: He continues to be a critical pathway to economic opportunity and future readiness for young New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And this year, DYCD is expanding career-ready programs to 260 schools, up from 230 in 2025, while continuing high school credit and career-ready CUNY reach

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: so that more students can earn high school or college credit through participation.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: The program also continues to prioritize young people facing barriers to employment, including NYCHA residents and youth living in priority precinct communities.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: In addition, DYCD is continuing initiatives launched in previous years, including SYEP Pride, in partnership with the Mayor's Office of Equity and Racial Justice and the New York

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: City Commission on Human Rights, which offers providers and employers training, supportive work experience, and professional development opportunities for participants.

chapters.summary: Escamilla-Davies reports the Fatherhood Program has engaged 500+ fathers, with a second annual Fatherhood Policy Symposium convening 100+ leaders. She announces the fifth annual Affirming Father convening on June 25th at John Jay College and thanks her staff.
chapters.title: Commissioner highlights Fatherhood Program and closes testimony

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And finally, the fatherhood program.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: To date, DYCT fatherhood program has engaged more than 500 fathers, supporting them as they navigate co-parenting, custody, and other complex family challenges,

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: while strengthening parent skills, family stability, and long-term father engagement.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: Through workshops, trainings, and supportive resources, these programs help fathers build connections with their children, families, and communities.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: Last month, DYCD hosted its second annual Fatherhood Policy Symposium, convening more than 100 leaders from government, academia, and community organizations.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: to highlight the critical role fathers play in well-being of children and families, and to advance father-friendly policies and practices across the city.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: On June 25th, DYCD will host its fifth annual Affirming Father convening at John Jay College of Criminal Justice to further elevate the importance of father engagement,

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: leadership, and community connection while continuing conversations about how systems and institutions can better support fathers and family across New York City.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And if I had more time, I would tout all of the other wonderful programs that this investment will yield for us, as well as this extraordinary staff that I have behind me and

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: working on the field right now that are doing everything they can to provide quality support to the network of providers that serve young people every day.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: Thank you again for the opportunity to discuss the fiscal 2027 executive budget, and we're looking forward to answering any questions you may have.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about $107.6M reduction in community development programs and $103.9M in contractual services. Officials explain the reduction is due to one-time council restoration funding for adult literacy that hasn't been added yet for FY27.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions DYCD on community development program reductions

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Great, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We've also been joined by Councilmember Brooks Powers.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I'm just going to dive right...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Councilmember Wong.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh wait, I think I said it the first time, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Here we go.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sure, Councilmember Felder.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: For the second time.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Such a riot.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so I'm gonna just dive right in so I can pass it off to my colleagues.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: For DYCD's FY27 budget in the executive plan,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: When comparing FY27 budget and the executive plan to FY26 adopted budget, we see that there's a reduction of $107.6 million in

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: community development programs, of which $103.9 million is contractual services.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So why is there a reduction in contractual services for community development programs?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What is the reason for the reduction of 1.6 million in full-time salary positions?

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: Good morning, Chair Lee.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: Nice to see you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Nice to see you.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: So the original reduction that you're referencing regarding is within the CD portfolio, and that's adult literacy, and that may be attributed to the one-time funding that we get annually for that portfolio, the restoration.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so, and then was the funding moved to other programs?

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: No, because it's a one-time restoration that we typically get from the council.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: One shot.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Oh, it's one shot, yeah, okay, got it.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the $3M vacancy reduction of 27 positions and which programs will be affected. Officials say locations haven't been identified yet but the goal is no service disruption. Lee also asks about Executive Order 12 savings, which came from surplus and unobligated funding with no service cuts.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions DYCD on vacancy reduction and savings targets

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then in terms of the vacancy reduction, a reduction of $3 million in FY26 to FY30 for a headcount of 27 in each fiscal year.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Which programs will see a headcount reduction and what is the amount for each?

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: At this time, we have not identified the locations where we'll have the reductions.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: We were just notified of it, and as you know, the agency has all our hands full with the implementation of these new initiatives.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: And so we intend to, in short order, start to look at where we will apply these reductions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: If you could keep us updated on that, that'd be awesome.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And is this just a reduced vacancy for each of the programs?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: If so, what is the remaining vacancy for each of the programs and the overall percentage?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know you're still working this out, but just some of the questions in terms of the alignment of where we're going.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then also, would this headcount reduction impact services provided by the agency?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Hopefully the answer is no.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: The answer is no.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: Ultimately, as the commission has indicated in our first hearing, she's doing an assessment of the

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: the organization and so at this time we have not necessarily identified the total impact of it but the ultimate goal is to ensure that there's no disruption to services and so we are confident that with the remaining vacancies that we have and our existing staff that we'll do our best to make sure that you

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: do appropriate oversight in the program areas and ensure contracts are registered and paid.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And what other savings, if any, were identified in the executive budget due to the mayor's Executive 12 order?

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: And so as part of the Executive 12 order, we looked at the new organization to see where we can find efficiencies and how funding is utilized.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: And we identified surplus as well as unobligated funding in a number of program areas.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: And with that, we submitted that and that was accepted.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: And as a result, we did not do any service cuts for any of our programs.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: And so we were just prioritizing that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Lee asks for the breakdown of school-based versus center-based Compass budgets and whether contracts will be registered by the school year. Officials commit to timely registration, with 50% of contracts already in process and a plan for cash flow support.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions DYCD on Compass RFP funding breakdown and contract registration

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then just really quickly moving over to Compass, because I know you all have been putting a lot of effort into the new RFPs.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I got to say, I started my, I did my first year social work degree at a Beacon program, so I just want to plus one to what you're saying.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But I know you said there was $727 million total for the RFPs, and so what's the breakdown with the school-based versus the center-based budgets?

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: I don't have that offhand.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: I can get back to you on that.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: I don't want to give you wrong information, but I can get that to you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And now that you have provided the list of new providers who will be entering the Compass system, does the agency feel there's enough time to...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I think I processed the contracts by the beginning of the FY27 school year.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know that's a challenge that you guys are trying to push through.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So just wondering if you could speak to that.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: Thank you for the question.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: We are committed to ensuring that the contracts are registered on time.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: We have a team that's dedicated to moving with the providers.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: We've had sessions with the providers to ensure that they are clear on the documents that are needed.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: We're working very closely with MOX as well as the Comptroller's Office to expedite registration.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: Know that the timeline is very, very tight, and we're planning to, you know, the biggest thing for providers is cash flow, so we are making sure that we have a plan in place for those that may not be registered on time.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: So we are definitely committed to getting them registered as quickly as possible.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And you've got to include OMB in that conversation, too, in the Comptroller's Office, because we've got to get those contracts registered quickly.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And what is the target date for completing the new contracts, and does the agency foresee any obstacles in meeting the target date?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know you just mentioned some of those things, but...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What's like the final sort of deadline that you guys are hoping to complete?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: Currently for our timely registration, we have about at least 50% of our contracts already in our pay department for registration.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: For Compass, for center-based, about 30% are already getting processed.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: So we're confident that we're moving forward.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: In the next week or so, our school-based awardees will start getting their contracts in the next couple of weeks.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: So we're doing what we can, and we're hopeful that by a month or so, all providers will have their contracts and we'll be moving forward.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And what was the final number of applications received for both the center-based and the school-based providers?

chapters.summary: Lee asks for total applications received. Officials report 209 center-based proposals with 121 awarded, 228 unique school-based providers with 147 awarded, and 3,500 individual school proposals. The minimum viable score was 70, based on organizational experience, staffing, community partnership, approach, and budget.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions Compass RFP application numbers and scoring criteria

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: I'm going to defer to our youth services team to provide that information.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then sort of the second part to that question, which I'll just ask, is what percentage of the center-based providers who submitted an application received an award?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So the first part is just total number of applications received for both.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: Thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: I think it was roughly 209 center-based proposals.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: Forgive me if I'm a little off.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: And we had proposals from roughly 3,500, if you consider each of the individual schools, because we had a community district.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: competition, it was many fewer than that, and we can get back to you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then, okay, so 209, and then how many of those that submitted were awarded for the center-based?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Right, 121.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, out of the 209.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And what percentage of school-based providers received an award?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So 3,500 applicants.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then it was, sorry, what was the number?

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: We made 806 awards in the school-based competition.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yes, and then there were, as you said, 3,500 that applied, right?

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: 3,500 individual school proposals, but many fewer if we count them by the community district proposals.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: Just to add, sorry, for school-based, it was 228 providers, unique providers that applied.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: 147 were awarded.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: For school-based?

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: For school-based.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then what was the criteria used to score the Compass RFP applicants and what was deemed as an unviable score?

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: So the criteria was organizational experience, service requirement, staffing, community partnership, approach, and budget.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: Those are the criteria that we looked at, and the minimum score was a 70.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Minimum was 70, okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks how DYCD is responding to providers who lost contracts. Commissioner Escamilla-Davies acknowledges the financial and operational impacts, details briefings being conducted, extended protest period, and transition planning efforts.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens questions DYCD on supporting providers who lost Compass awards

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know there's going to be a lot more questions on that, so I will leave it to my colleagues.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Chair Stevens?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I will jump right into it, and I know we are kind of pressed for time, so I just ask that the answers be succinct, because we want to make sure we get to as many people.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So I'll stay with the Compass theme and just kind of just thinking about just the impact of organizations, and as we know, these decisions aren't being made easily for you guys, but just thinking about...

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Because of this RFP, we know that some of these organizations that didn't get contracts, this is actually going to be a huge impact on their agencies.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so I wanted to know how is the agencies responding?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: How are you guys responding to the providers?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: What supports have you been giving them?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: What has this transition been looking like?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Especially for those we know that it's going to have big impacts where they didn't get any awards.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: So we're in the protest phase for the school base, so I'm going to speak more generally.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: First, I want to say that the non-awardies, I want to acknowledge that they have financial and operational impacts that are going to force them to make really difficult decisions, and we don't take that lightly.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And as I mentioned before, and this was a procurement process that followed all of the guidelines of procurement law, and yet we are going to try to stand in the gap for these providers.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: We're conducting briefings for them.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: We've extended, based on their feedback, the protest period so that everybody can get a briefing before the date, before they send a protest letter.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: But also we are trying to figure out about having conversations about what this is going to look like and if there are other things that we could do to support them.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And then really leaning into the transition periods and figuring out what does transition look like from summer to September?

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: How do we help transition providers in ways that hold their dignity intact while entering new providers and making the way for good providers to also stand in that gap and support children, youth, and families?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks about the plan for providers running Summer Rising who will lose their host sites after summer. Officials describe coordinating closeout processes, transition convenings between CBOs, and connecting with school leaders and communities.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens questions Summer Rising transition for providers losing Compass sites

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And just even thinking about summer, because I know you brought up summer.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So as we're thinking about summerizing, just going to record again.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Everybody knows I don't like that program.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Anyway, I just always have to say it.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: But anyway, wanting to think about the...

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: who would be running and operating Summer Horizon and then not having a host site going back and moving forward.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: What's the plan around what does ZYCD plan to support those providers who programs are closing out like right after that and and also just kind of considering what the morale for some of these programs going to going to be for this summer and closing out.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: So I think, thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: It's a good question because there's two parts to this, right?

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: There is the contracting closeout process, but we want to make this process as humane as possible.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: So our teams will be coordinating with those CBOs to have conversations on the process itself from the contracting side, from the financial side, and also that transition between CBO to CBO,

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: coordinating transition convenings between CBOs, connecting with school leaders and community to, again, really emphasize, like, this is not just about the contract, it's about the people on the site as well.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks for specifics on transition meetings, toolkits, and involving local council members. Officials describe transition meetings, knowledge transfer, staff retention discussions, family communication, and principal-level engagement, committing to involve council members.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens presses on transition process details and council member involvement

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Could you talk to me a little bit about what the transition processes look like?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: I know that's one of my biggest things, like how are we working with organizations on this transition process and what is that going to look like?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: You know, especially where, you know, there's cases where they might not have gotten that site, but they have another site.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And so, yes, we would love for those.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Folks who work in the program to stay, but if some people are going to go to the new sites, and I just think like this reshuffling is going to cause a little bit of a chaos, and so just trying to understand like how are we preparing,

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Not only the providers, but schools and families around this transition and what it's going to look like.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Do we have like documents?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Are we like working on like toolkits?

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: And then also how will we be involving the council members who I know a lot of them are already getting a lot of calls and pushback and want to make sure that the local council members are part of those conversations with these transition processes.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: So that's a great segue because part of this process is a real coordinated effort between all those stakeholders for, again, the best

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: transition as possible because the timeline is difficult and there's a lot of concerns about incoming change, right?

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: So the first part is there's core components of the plan that focus on transition meetings between all the different stakeholders, knowledge transfer.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Have the transition meetings already started to take place?

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: Yeah, starting to take place, right?

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: We're also keeping in mind that we are in a protest and appeal period, right?

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: And to make sure that we're coordinating all those engagements, as well as being thoughtful about them in that process

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: in a respectful way, thinking about what staff retention may look like, connecting with the school leaders,

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: family and school communication, and coordinating with New York City Public Schools centrally, and doing district-level engagement and principal-level engagement, because some instances, it's a good change that they're really anticipating for and they've been working for a long time towards.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: And then what our role is really kind of that central point to lift and escalate if there are some challenging conversations along the way and what we'll be tracking along that path.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: So one thing that we already started is engaging with those new CBOs and welcoming them in and really thinking about like readiness check.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: Where are they as an organization?

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: Where are they in terms of the staffing supports, coordination, what types of activities they're looking towards doing, connecting them with the school leader.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: So every single principal as part of the announcement was provided as who the CBO was and the contact information and coordinating with public schools where there are some instances where there's some disconnect.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks how 5,000 additional Compass slots for fall 2027 will be located and whether there will be another RFP. Escamilla-Davies says strategic decisions are being made to stabilize the ecosystem and leverage slots to mitigate impact.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens questions 5,000 additional Compass slots for FY28

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: So that's what we want to ensure throughout the.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I know it is a fast turnaround time, but again, I just want to make sure that we are tapping in the local council members to be a part of those conversations so that when we,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: so we could all be on the same page because it starts to get this conjoined when you have parents reaching out and the schools and all these things.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I want to make sure that local council members are part of the conversations and being brought in to be as supportive as possible with these transitions because I think, you know, a lot of folks are not happy.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And, you know,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We are in a place where some of these decisions are final.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so thinking about how do we work together to get not only the communities acclimated, but also the council members with some of these new organizations that will be coming into their communities.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: This is a community effort.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: So all state care holders involved.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: We'll definitely be connecting with you guys.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: And if there's any questions or flags, feel free to ask.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah, absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So according to the after school expansion plan, 5,000 additional campus slots will be added in fall 2027 or fiscal 2028 in addition to the slots in this RFP.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: How will you determine where those slots will be located?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What is the timeline to determining providers and beginning those slots?

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: We're in the process of making strategic decisions about how to leverage those slots, specifically to continue to stabilize the ecosystem and strengthening it as well.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Will there be an impact on providers who are currently providing after-school program services?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So thinking about as you're thinking about those expansions and even thinking about some of the mitigating factors now, how are we looking to leverage some of the losses that we have now, like moving forward?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Is there going to be another RFP?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Like, what does that look like?

chapters.summary: Stevens expresses strong opposition to closing any community-based centers, especially with 362 youth in secure detention. She asks about additional OMB funding to fill gaps. Officials confirm ongoing conversations with OMB about responding to concerns.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens questions community-based center closures and OMB gap funding

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: I would say we are going to move in ways that honor the current procurement process, but also that we are going to be opportunistic about ways to leverage.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: The current slots or the future slots in ways that help to mitigate some of the impact.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Post-award announcement, there are 10-day appeal process with a submission date of April 24th for the census-based providers that may not have been viable to receive awards.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Has there been any changes in this appeal process?

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: We extended the appeal process, I believe, by two weeks in order to have providers be able to receive a briefing before they made their appeal.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I know with the community base, it's a little different than it is for the school base.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And one of my biggest issues with the community base was that we were going to be closing programs.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And in a time where we are seeing young people having lack of programs, I'm like, well, why would we be closing any programs?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So can we talk about what that process was?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I know...

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: You guys have been really thoughtful about thinking about creative ways of like trying to work with different providers, but like especially with community.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I think in a moment of trying to close any center in this moment sounds crazy to me, especially going into the summer.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I'm just trying to get a better understanding where we are with that and how do we move forward.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: We're in the process of looking at the impact and making strategic decisions about how we move forward because we're in the process of investigating that right now and looking into it deeply.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: I don't have a formal strategy to share right now.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Has there been any additional funding for OMB to fill the gaps for these community centers?

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: I think there were an ongoing conversations that the commissioner just mentioned, recognizing the impact of the most recent awards.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: And we are looking into how we can appropriately respond to the concerns of all stakeholders.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: And so we're in ongoing conversations with OMB.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, I'll join those ongoing conversations because again, I just want to just say like, you know,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Community-based, everybody knows community-based programs has a special place in my heart.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And that's because they do a different type of work.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And the same students that show up at a school-based program typically does not show up at a community center.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And those are a lot of times are the kids we need to be reaching, right?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Community center staff are the ones that are typically out from the community, out on the ground and in there.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so for me in this moment, these community-based programs has to be a priority.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And that has been one of the biggest things that I've been pushing for.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I think like as we're thinking about where we need to save, and I understand that savings, we cannot do it on the back of young people.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And that is one thing that I'm just not okay with.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I've been saying this, my theme today is like, if we're going to have an affordable city, it needs to be affordable for young people.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And these are the things that we need, especially when we had ACS before you guys, and we have 362 young people in secure detention currently.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And that is abysmal because when I got into office, we were at 150.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so those numbers have.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: tripled at this point and closing community centers just does not sound like that is something we should be doing in this moment.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I'm going to continue to advocate and push because community-based programs need to be open and we should be expanding that work and not closing any programs.

chapters.summary: Stevens asks about the appeal process for school-based providers and 12 schools without awards. Officials confirm the same 10-day appeal process, extended for school-based providers, and explain the 12 schools had no viable or submitted proposals, with plans being finalized.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens questions school-based appeal process and unawarded schools

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I have a couple of questions about the appeal process for the school-based providers.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Is the appeal process the same for the school-based providers that they may, if they were not deemed viable, is it still 10 days as well for them as well?

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: Yes, so anyone that was not selected for an award, whether center-based or school-based, has the right to appeal.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And it's the same process as well?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And was there an extension for them as well?

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: So for center-based, there wasn't an extension.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: For the school-based, we gave the extension in the volume.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And a question even around the contracts.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Has every contract been awarded already?

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: So for school base, the majority of the contracts were awarded.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: There were 12 schools right now that currently were not awarded that we're working on getting.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And what does that process look like and why weren't they awarded?

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: So within that competition, there was either no viable proposal or no one was submitted a proposal for that particular school.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: So we're right now in conversations to finalize the plan for the 12.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so what does that plan look like and how is that going to work?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Is it going to go back out to procurement?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Are we looking at some of the providers that are already in the pool?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'm just trying to get an understanding of like how do we then

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Like, what does the mitigation for that look like?

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: It's still under review for consideration because our ultimate priority is to ensure that services are happening for the fall.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: We want to make sure that there's no lapse.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: So we are looking to see what's the best interest of the city to get services started in the fall.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah, because I just want to go on record for saying there was a lot of providers who got multiple contracts.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so if they are the people getting additional contracts, I'm going to be upset because I think capacity is one of the things that, you know, we know that a lot of these folks will say they have the capacity.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then when it's time to produce, it starts to get watered down.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I want us to really look at some of these groups who might not have been able to get awarded because there was a lot of competition in certain areas and things like that to kind of mitigate that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But thinking about capacity and being able to, trying to keep some of these providers whole should, and I would like to see that be a priority.

chapters.summary: Stevens raises concerns about new providers getting school-age child care licenses from DOHMH by September. Officials report about 200 programs need new licenses, with coordinated inspections, technical assistance, and contingency plans in place.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens questions SAC licensing for new Compass providers

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: How's the agency assessing providers who do not have school age child care licenses?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Because that's another question, especially for the new providers that's coming in that's going to be in these places.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: One of the things I was showing out about when the RFP came out was just like, are we giving additional supports to DOHMH?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Because they are a huge part in this process that we kind of forget about.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so I'm trying to understand, especially with these new programs coming in, how do we expect to get all these people cleared?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Because even if you were cleared at another site, you got to do the process over.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And with the SAC requirements, I'm really concerned about folks being able to start and being able to get up and going by, what is it, September?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: August?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What is it?

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: September.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: September.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: Yep.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: So thank you, Chair.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: That's a great question.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: At the start of the RFP planning process, we've been in full engagement with the OHMH and New York City Public Schools because there's a whole coordinated process that happens behind the scenes outside of the application process, which includes inspections, the facilities itself.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: So we wanted to really focus on the more trouble spots areas and then on the application side, work with those new and existing CBOs to ensure that they are fully understanding the process itself, that we have support and technical assistance that we can provide on our end as well.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: What's been great is that there's only a small subset of these programs starting the fall that actually require a new license itself.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: So how many right now we're looking behind the scenes outside of the application process, which includes inspections, the facilities itself.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: Looking at just over 200.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: So that's a share.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: Again, it's awful.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: So we wanted to really focus on the more trouble spots areas and then on the application side, work with those new and existing CBOs to ensure that they are fully understanding the process itself, that we have support and technical assistance that we can provide on our end as well.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: You said 200 like it was like 10.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: But let's be clear.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: 200 out of the total 900 plus programs we have.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: We've done this in the past when we did the Sonic expansion.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I was there, and it was not smooth.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: What's been great is that there's only a small subset of these programs starting the fall that actually require a new license itself.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It was not smooth.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: Smoother.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I was there.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It was not smooth, and it was a mess.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: And you're 100% correct on that.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: And what's been great since then is that there actually is an online portal, right?

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: So how many right now we're looking?

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: We had to go from paper-based to online since then.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: So there's been a lot of progress since then.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And there's a lot of hiccups in there, too.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: You know I know the process.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: And that is the process of what we do here at DYCD to make sure that we can make those troublesome spots as comfortable as possible, right?

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: I think this is an opportunity for us to really think about these processes across multiple agencies and how we're tracking them and ensuring that we are escalating.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: As we speak right now, we have these teams coordinating and meeting to discuss where additional supports are going to be needed, really coordinating with public schools and identifying the classrooms so the OHMH can go out and do the inspections in a quick manner and engaging with CBOs that still need support in that process.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Is there a contingency plan if they are not able to get their SAC license and up and running?

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: Yes, there will be plans in place.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So you have that plan already?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so you'll share that with me?

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: Yes.

chapters.summary: Stevens requests lists of non-awarded school-based providers, non-viable center-based providers, and funded groups with amounts. Officials confirm they can provide all requested lists.
chapters.title: Chair Stevens requests detailed lists of Compass RFP outcomes

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: All right, great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Can the agency provide the city council with a list of current school-based providers who did not get awards, a new RFP, a list of school-based programs who did not get awarded?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: the new RFP with the old funded amount lines, a list of non-viable center-based providers, and a list of DYCD funded groups for both center-based and school-based awards and their amounts.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: You don't have to go through it if you guys can just get that to us.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yes, we can.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I have a lot of questions, but I'm going to pass it off.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I didn't even get to any of the other sections, so I'll be back.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So thank you.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: She will be back for round two.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Maloney raises concerns about Manhattan Youth losing contracts at some schools despite 94% attendance and positive audits. Officials explain each competition is evaluated on its own merit with different evaluators and competition levels.
chapters.title: Councilmember Maloney questions Manhattan Youth non-renewal in Compass RFP

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: So we're going to move to council member questions.

utterances.speaker: Althea V. Stevens
utterances.text: Council member Maloney, followed by deputy speaker.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: On behalf of countless parents and principals across my district, and you're going to be hearing from other districts in Manhattan, I have concerns about the new Compass RFP,

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: and in particular Manhattan Youth, which was discontinued at some schools, but not all schools, with limited transparency about why and limited engagement with the parents and the principals on the ground, to Councilmember

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Stevens'point earlier.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: This is a program that had a 94% attendance rate, positive audits, and was recommended first by many principals.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: So the question is, why were they deemed insufficient?

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And if their program truly was insufficient, then why were they awarded for some contracts and not others, including a case where there's a co-located school where Manhattan Youth was awarded for one school and not.

utterances.speaker: DYCD Official
utterances.text: The other.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: I want to say that, you know, I want to speak more broadly about the selection process, and I'm going to hand it over to Dana.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: But I do want to talk about this in terms of transparency and the narratives that are out there.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: This was a procurement process that was highly transparent.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: The concept paper was co-developed by the field.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And in any procurement, there are winners and non-winners.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And my heart goes out to all of the agencies that were not awarded.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: But I am really proud of the rigorous process that we engaged in and the level of care that DYCD took in making these awards.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: I want to speak about that, but I also want to give you the technical answer, which I'm going to hand over to Dana.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And I also want to say that every single competition in every single region was competing with other providers.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And so they were ranked in that geographical area accordingly.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: So there were some providers that in some areas they were rewarded, awarded their specific, they were re-awarded, and in other areas they were not.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And I can tell you on the provider side, that is, as someone who's been a provider, that is devastating, and yet it is part of the procurement process.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: I think the thing I want to highlight, because this has not been procured for over 10 years, there is a sense of ownership that I think is really felt in terms of relationships.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: in terms of continuity.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And yet what we know to be true is that procurement is necessary because it allows for innovation, for equity, and for accountability.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: So I think more than one thing could be true about this process.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: But again, I share the deep empathy and regard for the staff, for the families and the children, youth that are going to have a new provider.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: But I'm also really excited about the providers that were selected that are high quality, high caliber, and that offer really great services.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: Thank you, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: To add, I definitely want to push back a little on the fact that the procurement wasn't transparent.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: It was extremely transparent.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: We had made selections based on a basis for award where there was criteria outlined on how we were making the awards.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: In the case of Manhattan Youth, they did apply to multiple competitions, and each competition is handled on its own merit.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: So you could have one school, which was considered a competition, be more competitive than others, and we made selections based on where scores, ranking, provided diversity.

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: There were many areas that we looked at when making those selections, but it stood behind the basis for award that was released in the RFP, and that was a procurement document that was made public.

chapters.summary: Maloney asks for details on how Manhattan Youth scored against criteria and whether principals who ranked them first were overruled. Officials say specifics can't be shared during the protest period but note over 80% of principals got their first or second choice.
chapters.title: Councilmember Maloney presses on Manhattan Youth scoring and principal input

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I have one more question.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: You shared what the technical factors are that you were looking at.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: One of them was around budget.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: Can you share more details on how Manhattan Youth scored against those criteria?

utterances.speaker: Dana
utterances.text: At this time, we can't give specifics because we are in the protest period.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And also, they will be awarded a briefing, so they will get not only their scores and how they fared in relationship to other people, but they will get also specific feedback on their RFP of areas that were highlighted by the evaluator.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And are there instances where a school and a principal did rank Manhattan Youth as excellent and it was still not awarded again?

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And if that's the case, there's a process where the principals on the ground are being overruled in a closed-door process, if you could speak to that.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: It wasn't a closed-door process.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: It was actually a transparent process that was guided by a contribution that was made by the principal.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: So principals'selections was weighed, but the primary selections, as Dana mentioned, were the RFP.

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And so I'm proud to say that over 80% of principals got their first or second award.

chapters.summary: Maloney asks about recourse if new providers don't meet community needs, noting lost services like sports and arts. Escamilla-Davies expresses confidence in new providers' responsiveness and commits to taking necessary action if they underperform.
chapters.title: Councilmember Maloney asks about recourse for disrupted services

utterances.speaker: Sandra Escamilla-Davies
utterances.text: And with principals that did not get their first ranking, we're going to be working to help that transition on the ground to really think about ways to be a bridge and to make sure that they are centering this choice around children, youth, and families.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: And my last question, I know I'm out of time here, is if there's a chance to re-evaluate these decisions, and we're signing up for a six-year RFP, so if these

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
utterances.text: programs are not meeting the needs of the community.

utterances.speaker: Virginia Maloney
