chapters.summary: Encarnación asks which MOIA programs are RFPed. Officials say no new RFPs for FY27, with focus on extending contracts to avoid service breaks. They describe evaluation metrics including number served, languages spoken, and types of services, varying across 11 initiatives.
chapters.title: Chair Encarnación questions MOIA RFP status and program evaluation metrics

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: This is where it gets confusing.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: This is where I'm not really understanding.

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: So we'll get into that because I'm trying to understand like, so then as far as directives, mission, who tells them what they should be doing on a day-to-day basis, is that coming from a different agency?

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: Is it coming from your vision?

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: If it's coming from your vision, then it makes sense that it was

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: It's your staff, right?

utterances.speaker: Elsie Encarnación
utterances.text: If it's coming from DSS or DCAS, then it's their staff.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: Yeah, Chair, you raise a very great question, and we're trying to demystify it, so I apologize if we're adding to the confusion.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: The staff that are currently at MOIA, even if their budget comes from a different agency, they are executing the work and the mission of MOIA.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: They are physically in MOIA's offices.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: I see them, I interact with them.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: Their core responsibilities are to implement our programs, our policies.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: So the total PS budget.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: budget across the agencies, including the mayor's office, is about $5.3 million for staffing that implement our program and our work.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: That is actually really helpful.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I just wonder why the hesitancy to just call it Moya's budget in that way, because then

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I can see growth there and I can see the importance behind the mayor's office of immigrant affairs when we're talking about the millions of dollars for a budget versus barely a million dollars for the office, but we'll move on.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So under,

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Which MOIA programs are RFPed and which are not RFPed?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So most immediately for FY27, we're not RFPing any new programs.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We, especially with the added baseline funding, our focus has been to extend contracts, right, to avoid break-in services.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We're still in the process of making a determination and an assessment on whether to RFP programs in the future.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We're still in that process.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And once we make that decision and we move forward, if we do move forward right with that decision, we'll definitely ensure that community-based organizations and all the information is public with

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: plenty of time for folks to be able to participate as part of that procurement process.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Can you talk about some of the metrics that you're going to use to evaluate those programs briefly?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Like, what are you looking for?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: How do you?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: See success and extending those programs?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: On the current programs that we have, I can, well, there is a variety of things.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But I'll try to answer the RFP question and then I think to the programs that we currently have.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I think that's, again, conversations that we will have as the commissioner has been mentioning.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We have been.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: actively meeting with all of our providers to hear the good, the bad, and the ugly.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And those conversations will continue in regards to what's working and what's not.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: What we have pride ourselves with, and I've heard this from Chair Lee as well, is we have tried to maintain our programs, some of them very nimble, so providers can respond in real time.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And then in regards to just the evaluations, it's an array of things.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: It's a combination of how many folks have been served, the types of folks that have been served, languages spoken, things to that extent.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But it varies for, I think the number's at 11 right now of the different initiatives that we have.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But it's also a point that we're open to having conversations in regards to anything that you might be hearing from providers yourself or things that you're learning on the ground in regards to what we should be thinking about as well.

chapters.summary: Encarnación asks what the $33M added in the prelim will be used for. Ali explains it continues current programming while expanding key initiatives, with $14.6M directed to the Rapid Response Legal Collaborative and legal support centers for deportation defense, plus habeas project funding.
chapters.title: Chair Encarnación questions $33M in legal services funding allocation

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And are they currently in a contract cycle, and what year are they in most of these RFPs?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So I can start.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I don't know if you want me to walk you through the different programs that we have, but the legal support centers, they were part of an RFP that happened last year, and this is their first year of a three-year contract, so that's one bucket.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We have HRI partners that are in their renewal cycle now and are starting again this year, same contracted partners.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We have our immigrant rights workshop, same thing, as well as our hotline, which will continue with Catholic Charities, the rapid response to your collaborative.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The commissioner mentioned there was an enhancement there, but that contract is also moving forward.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Our English language support centers as well, those contracts continue.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I think I got all of them, but the only one I think that you can think of within the RFP cycle would be our legal support centers, which are locked into a three-year contract.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Got it.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Speaking of immigration legal services, you know, we're very excited to see roughly 33 million

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: comprised of 11.6 million added in the prelim for legal support centers and 21.3 million added for the expansion of immigrant legal services baseline starting in fiscal 2027.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But there seemed to be, and there was no additional funding added in the executive plan.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I wanted to know what exactly will this additional funding added in prelim be used for?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: For example, which existing programs will be expanded?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Will there be new programs?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: The additional funding is going to allow us to continue the current programming while also expanding key support initiatives that we know the immigrant New Yorkers are depending on.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: As part of the funding that was baselined, we know that there is still a growing demand for immigration defense services.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: The city increased in baseline.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: funding for the RLC by $3 million.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: You know, our understanding is that as we've engaged more with partners and organizations that maintaining the support and growing that support is important with the baseline funding.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: I think having the continuity of service has been the biggest plus here.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: And the other thing I want to uplift, especially when it comes to our rapid response legal collaborative work, is that

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: because of the fast-changing federal policies, it forces, and the changes in law as well, it forces the immigration attorneys to have to sort of chase all of those changes and be nimble.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: And we're really proud that we were able to baseline some of this support, get additional resources in FY26 to make sure that they have what they need to actually be nimble in that space.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So it's an expansion.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It's not creating new programs.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Will any new programs be created?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: Yeah, we're absolutely looking forward to, you know, having those conversations in the foreseeable future about, you know, I think the biggest important thing for me has been, you know, we've mentioned meeting with all of our providers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: It was like a week-long marathon meeting of hundreds of providers specifically to understand

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: not just what the needs are, but like are the programs that we are currently administering having an impact in the communities and how are we measuring that?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: So those conversations for future programming are important.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: For us to have.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: It just requires a little bit more community engagement and stakeholder input.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And so what specific immigration cases or clients is the $33 million anticipated to cover?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: Sorry, I didn't grab that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: You said the $33 million?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: We're making significant investment in deportation defense.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: MOIA is directing $14.6 million of that to go to the RLC, to our legal support centers, and to support New Yorkers who are facing deportation cases.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: I think that level of investment really shows the urgency.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: And my colleague just passed a note that we do want to uplift that there is additional resources that will be going to our habeas project.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: And we can provide details on the breakdown of that.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: Particular funding.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: That would be very helpful.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about planning for potential federal clawbacks. Ali says city funding shields programs from direct federal clawbacks but MOIA tracks trickle-down effects. When asked about budget reserves for emergencies, Ali says she needs to circle back.
chapters.title: Chair Encarnación questions federal clawback risks and budget reserves

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And do you know if any of this new funding will be allocated directly to vendors?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: All of it.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: All of it?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Perfect.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And have you thought about, and I'm sure you have thought about this, so that part is rhetorical, but

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: how are you thinking about and planning for potential federal clawbacks of legal service funding or restrictions on who can be served?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: Yeah, it's one of the things that keeps you up at night.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: But I think it's really important to highlight that our legal services are funded by the city, which shields them from direct federal clawbacks.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: However, we are actively tracking what can be trickle-down effects from federal funding cuts and restrictions on whom our organizations and providers can serve.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: We remain, like I said, in close communication with our agency partners regarding federal policy changes and any threats to accessing services.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: Specifically, we've been talking to our partners and agencies around the mixed status rule change that was being proposed by HUD.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: And, of course, staying in close communication with our providers who they flag emerging issues that they see on the ground regularly.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Do you know if there will be like budget reserves for those kind of emergency situations or something that would happen, you know, it could be overnight with this federal administration?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Are there any kind of reserves specifically for MOIA?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: Yeah, that's that's.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: That's something that I would have to circle back with you on.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: I'm unsure.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about advocacy with ONA and state leaders. Ali describes coordinating with ONA on the family preparedness phone bank and ongoing partnership to prevent service gaps. Lee then asks about Rapid Response Legal Collaborative service numbers.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions MOIA advocacy with state Office of New Americans

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I know, can you talk a little bit about some of the advocacy you've done with the Office of New Americans and the state leaders to advocate for some of the services and the funding for legal services specifically, but

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: anywhere you've done some of that advocacy?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: Yeah, this is a priority that we're actively working to address.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: I mentioned earlier, most recently, MOIA coordinated with ONA on our family preparedness phone bank, and we're going to continue to build on that partnership to really

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: identify and respond to whatever additional needs may arise.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: I think for us, a priority is to make sure that we're preventing any sort of gaps in services that may arise and are looking forward to continuing that partnership with ONA.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you for that.

chapters.summary: Officials report 383 students supported through KYR efforts, 90 individuals screened under expanded DOE scope with 50 receiving legal services in Q2-Q3, and 137 screenings through the main RLC contract. Lee notes the numbers seem low; Ali explains turnout challenges for events.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions Rapid Response Legal Collaborative service numbers

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And in the rapid response legal collaborative, you've talked a little bit about that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: How many, can you tell me how many people have been served through the work for fiscal 2026 thus far?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yes, give me one.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I'm just gonna get the numbers.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We came with numbers just in case.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Love that.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So through 20, this year to date, I can tell you that on some of the KYR efforts that we've done, there have been 383 students supported, grades nine through 10.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: In quarter two and quarter three of FY26, the collaborative screened about 90 individuals under the expanded DOE scope and provided legal services to 50 of them.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And I'm going to circle back on the other bucket because there's two buckets of work that I just want to have in front of me.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: The one that I walked you through is connected to the rapid response to your collaborative in schools.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah, it seems pretty low the numbers and maybe I just in skewing.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So can you talk about maybe what have you heard are some of the biggest challenges in supporting our students at that level?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: Yeah, I think one of the biggest challenges right now is turnout for virtual and those in-person events.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: We're working really closely with our partners to look into how do we turn out for some of those future events?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: What does outreach look like?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: How can we better ensure that individuals are receiving access to the information that they need?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Yeah, we've struggled with that, with the know your rights as well, so that rings very true.

chapters.summary: Lee asks who authored the EO 13 audit report and MOIA's role. Ali explains the Interagency Response Committee prepared it, chaired by the First Deputy Mayor with MOIA consultation. The audit found significant gaps in agency reporting on ICE interactions.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions EO 13 audit report authorship and findings

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And I'm going to get into the city's sanctuary policies across the agencies, Executive Order 13 and what happened there, and then I'm going to allow my colleagues to ask some questions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: For many of you who may not know yet, on February 6th, the mayor issued Executive Order 13 to strengthen the city's sanctuary protections.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And the report for this executive order audit and its findings was published online just recently on May 22nd.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I want to thank the administration for giving the council a briefing on this before it was put online.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It was extremely helpful to hear about it before actually.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: reading it.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I had some questions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So who in the administration authored the report and what was your role throughout the before the findings came out?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: The report was prepared by the Interagency Response Committee, which, as you mentioned, it was created by Executive Order EO-13.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: It's chaired by the FDM.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: It includes direct consultation from MOIA, our Chief Counsel's Office, and the Special Advisor for Strategic Coordination.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: That's who prepared the report.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: We meet and have met throughout this process in leading up to the release of the audit quite regularly to discuss how are we going to roll out this information.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: I was pleased to see that we had such great participation from our partners in the council for the briefing as well.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: It was great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then moving forward, will Moya be kind of like the lead of those conversations and the ongoing audits, for lack of a better word here?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: Yeah, Moya is a member of the IRC in a leadership capacity, so we will be continuing our role in that process.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: In that report, the audit reports and talks about significant gaps in city agency reporting on interactions with federal immigration authorities.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about the significant gaps identified. Ali explains many interactions weren't reported due to ineffective tracking, incidents surfacing after reports were published, or guidance not requiring reporting of subpoenas and warrants. New guidance now requires over-inclusive reporting.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions EO 13 audit gaps and reporting improvements

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Can you describe in a little bit of detail what those significant gaps were?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: Yeah, the audit revealed

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: that many interactions between city agencies and federal immigration authorities

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: were not reported at all, either because agencies weren't effectively tracking those interactions because incidents surfaced maybe after relevant reports had already been published or because the existing guidance

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: didn't require reporting interactions such as subpoenas and warrants and other instances where federal agents forcefully entered city property.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: And I think to directly address these gaps, the mayor has updated and approved additional guidance that will be issued by the IRC.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: And in this guidance, reporting requirements have been strengthened and clarified, including by expanding the types of interactions that are subject to that reporting.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: In short, agencies are now directed to be over-inclusive in what they report to MOIA.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And did you see any gaps from your own data analysis that maybe weren't in the

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: audit, the official audit, or that you may have put in there from your own analysis or just recognized were missing from the report?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: Yeah, you know, I think until now, the reports that were issued by MOIA, they categorize like other instances as any types of interaction between city agencies and federal immigration agencies that were not otherwise subject to reporting.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: So that was.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: As I mentioned earlier, it was something that we also had identified as a gap.

chapters.summary: Lee asks about a recommendation to send a patrol executive to 911 calls about ICE presence. Ali says she needs to consult with NYPD and IRC partners for details on what a patrol executive is. Lee asks about agency compliance with posting policies online.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions NYPD patrol executive recommendation from EO 13 audit

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And one of the recommendations submitted by the NYPD is to establish a protocol requiring

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: that the communications division notify operations of any 911 calls related to the presence of federal immigration authorities or immigration matters.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And the recommendation says that a patrol executive will be sent to the scene.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: What is a patrol executive?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: Yeah, that's a great question.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: That is information that we can share after we have more discussion with the NYPD and other IRC partners.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Do you know what I got as a uniform officer?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Do you know that it's going to be a separate person?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The wording is interesting.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: Yeah, that's something I'll have to get back to you on.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I'll save those follow-up questions for this patrol executive for that time.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then I know that EO 13, the six agencies had to make their policies and protocols regarding interactions with ICE available on their websites.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Do you know if that they have done so, if all of them have done so?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: Now that the mayor has received the audit and adopted the recommendations for changes to the policies and protocols, agencies are beginning to implement those changes and will complete that process in the coming months.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: The IRC is going to continue in our role as leadership to work with the agencies as they undertake this process, which also includes a public engagement component.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: You know, the executive order actually, in terms of a specific timeline, requires the agencies to promptly publish their policies online, and that's something that the IRC is working to ensure that the agencies, in fact, do.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: But there's no, like, set timeline of when, I mean, promptly.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: There's no deadline, but promptly.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And then who monitors the compliance?

chapters.summary: Before yielding to colleagues, Lee asks whether COLAs or rate increases can be addressed without new RFPs. Officials say they need to consult with their contracting agency and will follow up, as Lee notes this could be a way to increase provider funds.
chapters.title: Chair Lee questions COLA and rate increases without new RFPs

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: You said it's the IRC?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The IRC.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, so I'm going to take a break so I can let some of my colleagues ask some questions.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: I'll have Linda.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Actually, before that, I just had a really, really quick clarifying question on the RFP question that the chair just asked because

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: While I know there's pros and cons to not issuing out new RFPs, I guess my main concern, and correct me if I'm wrong, is that it also doesn't allow for new rates and increases, right?

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So I'm just wondering if there's something baked in or a process where COLAs or the rates per case and those types of things will be increased or looked at.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: For that, we're going to have to circle back with you.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We're going to have to consult with our contracting agency to get more details on that.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Okay, that would be great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Because if there's a way where we could address that issue, even if there's no new RFP, I think that would be great.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Because I know that that could be a way to increase some of the funds that they're getting.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: So, okay.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We have Councilmember Joseph followed by Brewer.

chapters.summary: Joseph asks about expanding the Haitian Response Initiative and partnering with council offices. Ali says providers remain the same at current funding. Joseph also asks about asylum seeker filing fees due May 29; officials describe funding allocated for fees and outreach to providers.
chapters.title: Councilmember Joseph questions HRI expansion and asylum seeker fee support

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Wonderful.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Good to see you always, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Just a couple of follow-up questions.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: I know you said you provided programming on the HRI.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Number of people served, 610 for 2026 thus far.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: That's correct.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Will there be an expansion of the HRI initiative?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Is that something you're looking?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Currently, the providers will be remaining the same at the same funding level.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: So I have another question.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Are you considering maybe thinking about partnering with council member offices to provide those services in their offices?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: That's something that was actually raised when we met with our HRI partners on how do we reach more deeper into communities.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: So it's part of an ongoing conversation.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Yeah, I think we should consider that.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: I know that's, how are you supporting asylum seekers?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: I noticed that they have a fee they have to pay by May 29, 2026.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: How are you getting that word out to the community?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Well, one, to credit the council and also some of the work that we do.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: We don't get enough praises.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Go ahead, though.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I have to be honest here, but because of the partnership that we had last year during the budget negotiations, we were able to allocate funding for fees,

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: including some of the filing fees that you're mentioning right now, which I think is a win overall.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We have been working with our providers to ensure that they're aware that that's available to them, and we have been pretty successful of spending some of that funding down.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: How do communities or, for example, my office or non-for-profit get in touch with you to know that these fees are available for members?

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Because I think today was the deadline for some of them to apply for those fees, for filing.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, we are always available after this hearing.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I'll send you an email, Councilmember, and then we're happy to make the connections with our legal service providers.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: I would love that.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Rita C. Joseph
utterances.text: Thank you, Chairs.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Councilmember Brewer?

chapters.summary: Brewer asks about Ruth Messenger's successful home health aide teaching program and whether it will expand. Ali describes a lengthy discussion with Messenger about scaling the model. Brewer also asks about asylum seeker population numbers and adult literacy funding.
chapters.title: Councilmember Brewer questions home health aide training program for asylum seekers

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: First of all, Commissioner, thank you for showing up at Open Arms District 3.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They very much appreciated that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Number two,

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: As you know, Ruth Messenger is very involved with people who are here seeking asylum.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And she has managed, thanks to money that you and previous Speaker Adrian Adams allocated to the Jewish hospital, to have a teaching program for homeowners.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Health aides.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Really successful.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So my question is, are you doing that elsewhere?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: In other words, it's my experience because I have folks who are seeking asylum in my house, that the job situation is what they're looking for.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Obviously, people are delivering, but they would like something more secure.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Unfortunately, we're all getting older, and home health aides are desperately needed.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So my question is, are there other such programs?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Will money be allocated?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Is it something you're looking toward?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: Yeah, thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: I actually had a very good and lengthy discussion with Ruth.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: I'm sure you did.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: Specifically about the success of this program, because it was a pilot, and talked a little bit about ways to expand that into other job opportunities as well.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: So it is part of an ongoing conversation that we are hoping to continue with Ruth on that specific teaching hospital, but also just using that model to see if it's something that we can scale up and support.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, and is there a sense of, I have no sense, I mean, I wish that every single person who came here was able to stay, who was seeking asylum.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We need the schools, we need them all.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That's not the case with the person in Washington.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So, my question is, do we have some sense of how many folks here are seeking asylum and who are, obviously not everybody's in shelter, not everybody is,

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: able to be found.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: You can't find the people in my house.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I bet.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So my question is, how do we have any sense of the numbers, many of whom could use these programs that Ruth is trying to establish?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I don't have that number handy, but we will get it, and we can circle back with that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay, because I think that would help us give some sense of where we're going in terms of those kinds of programs.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The young people I know just got jobs as security officers.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Very exciting, big deal, better than delivering, safer, et cetera, from their perspective, more steady.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Second question is, everybody has to learn English if you want to get a job.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Some of them are learning it from YouTube.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That seems to be a way.

chapters.summary: Brewer asks about adult literacy cuts and English learning programs. Officials describe MOIA's English Language Support Centers with $500K per library system, the We Speak program for beginners, and the importance of English learning for economic mobility.
chapters.title: Councilmember Brewer questions adult literacy and English language learning programs

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But the adult literacy, can you bring us up to date on cuts, where you're looking?

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Obviously, the feds have been very unfortunately cutting, and I think this is as important as all the other topics that we've been talking about today.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yeah, I can start.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I don't have, in regards to just overall cuts, we don't have that, but we are happy to say that via the Moya English Language and Learning Support Centers, we have added

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: and have extended in baseline funding for our English language support centers at 500K per library system.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: We do have what we call We Speak, which is a way for folks who might not be ready to join an adult literacy class, might be a bit too shy to speak out or speak openly and learn to try to learn the language.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: and encourages people to communicate amongst each other, but also learn about New York City civic engagement life and what other offices and agencies are doing around the city.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So it's a bit of a multi-fold way of integrating new New Yorkers into our city.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The reason I ask is it would be helpful because I know you bring information and agencies together to have a sense of where we are with, in general, the English ELLs because

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: it seems to me that if there are huge waiting lists, which there always have been, then that would be something that the council and the mayor should be looking at.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It is the one way that everybody can get jobs.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I'd love to know, again, it exists in different agencies, libraries, DOE, U, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So is that something that you can work on?

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: I mean, we know, and you kind of hit it on the nail with both of your questions, that English learning is the pathway for civic participation, as Loretta mentioned, but economic mobility and just long-term stability for immigrant communities

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: in our city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: Through our English language, English learning and support centers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: We know they're designed in a way to strengthen language skills, but really connect them, immigrant New Yorkers, to larger services.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: You know, we see this as an important, it's a value, it's an important program that we administer at MOYA, and we would love to continue those conversations with the council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Faiza Ali
utterances.text: on how to provide additional support for adult literacy.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Okay, thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Councilmember Aviles?

chapters.summary: Avilés asks about hotline usage trends and whether increased referrals strain legal support centers. Officials describe a capacity tracker tool for real-time coordination, decreased call volume but increased call duration from 4 to 7 minutes reflecting complexity, and screening to route calls appropriately.
chapters.title: Councilmember Avilés questions hotline usage increase and legal support center capacity

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Thank you, chairs.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Thank you all for being here.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: I have a couple of scattershot questions.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: In terms of the immigration legal support hotline, if I recall, it sounds like the number of calls that you've received, that there have been a good number of improvements to the hotline.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Do you have a sense of what the increase is in usage from the prior fiscal year?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yeah, I'm about to get these numbers, I know, because they're watching.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So I think we've talked a little bit about this in years past, but there was actually a decrease in some of the calls during the last administration.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And I think it was connected also to sort of the federal environment and things to that extent.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But that's just overall.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: I know that now we have seen an increase in calls in other languages, Haitian, Creole to be one, that is very different.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: And that change really began January of this year.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But I'll try to get you a number because I know that you might be interested in that.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Yeah, no, I think, you know, our perpetual struggle is to make sure that these hotlines are as effective as possible.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: And I think.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: You know, want to recognize that there have been investments and work

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: to improve those things.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: So I'm glad to see that, to hear that there are more languages incoming.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: That's a good sign.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: I think I was also heartened to hear about, I mean, the 12,000 of the 19,000 being particularly referrals for legal support.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Kind of leads me to my next question in terms of the legal support partners.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Obviously, they have, they're bound by contracts with certain outcomes.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: And the improvement in the hotline

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: will potentially mean more volume for the legal support centers who are bound by specific contracts and service limits.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Is there a consideration to adjust support to the legal centers?

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: As that volume increases in referrals?

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Because I guess what I'm getting at here is that I feel like the volume of referrals probably coming to the legal support centers has increased from every direction.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: And the contracts are stagnant and not increasing with the same level.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Obviously, it's not a one-to-one, we'd love that, but do you have a sense of how that is kind of metting out at the end of the day?

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Yeah, I can, and I also just want to clarify

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: that referrals are made to the legal support centers, the Rapid Response Legal Collaborative, Knife Up, as well as we were able to also provide connections to iCare as well.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So there's a bit more support with that.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: On the legal support center side, as you know,

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Besides the three-year contracts, there's other providers as well who have a one-year contract, and as the commissioner mentioned, that funding has been renewed.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So there's a bit of an expanded.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: But what we have done internally is we've created a capacity tracker as a centralized tool to support the hotline referrals and the legal support centers.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: So we're not just like sending people without consulting with the legal support centers.

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: Weekly we send out an act stem to provide us with a real-time snapshot of what their capacity looks like

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: then that really helps our hotline counselors as well as the legal support center so it's not uniform that we're like we have to take a hundred people

utterances.speaker: Speaker
utterances.text: we check in to see what their capacity is of that week and then we inform our hotline counselors in real time so they have that as well

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And part of our sort of ongoing work with the hotline, and because we also recognize that, you know, we've been doing a ton of outreach and are about to roll out more outreach.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And with this hotline number, what does that mean for capacity?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think part of the improvements that we have with Catholic Charities and some of our legal service providers is understanding better, how do we, what does the flow of information look like?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: How should legal service providers be receiving those calls?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think that there's been improvements being made to the coordination to just also address the capacity concern.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Our intention is not to flood the legal service providers with all calls, but screen them so the appropriate calls are being sent to the appropriate organizations.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Great.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: If I may add one thing, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The other note to make, just data-wise, is that the calls have, although we have received lesser calls, the call time and duration of the call has increased.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So from FY25, when the average was about four minutes, and FY26, it's about seven, which talks about just the complexity of the calls that we're getting as well.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So just wanted to add that as well.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: No, that's helpful.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I think I'm heartened to hear about the capacity tracker because it is a concern, I think, where we can be more targeted and precise in our understanding where the gaps are.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: In the field, then I think we'll be able to kind of build on that, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I'm heartened to hear that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: If I may, one quick.

chapters.summary: Avilés asks about MOIA's advocacy for the $20M Education for Integration and Equity proposal. Officials confirm they've received the proposal and been in touch with DYCD, with conversations ongoing. Avilés emphasizes the need for proactive planning against federal adult literacy cuts.
chapters.title: Councilmember Avilés questions adult literacy funding advocacy and $20M proposal

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I guess just we can talk some more about that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think we still have a whole three years with the Orange Chupacabra and a complex system, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: As we see every day, more and more changes are being made, which brings more complexity to the legal field.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I think there's just going to be an ongoing issue to make sure that we check in and be very precise with how we're supporting the building of the capacity.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: To find lawyers.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: In terms of adult literacy, I'm sorry I didn't, I think some of these questions probably for DYCD, but I'd like to know if MOYA is

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: advocating for the new proposal around the $20 million for the, called the Education for Integration and Equity Program.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: An important allocation.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'd just like to know what those conversations have been like.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We have been in touch with and have received a proposal that has been put out for the $20 million.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think I see some folks in the space who are here now.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And we have touched base with DYCDs, but those conversations are ongoing.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I don't have anything new to share, but happy to.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We will be listening in to the DYCD hearing as well.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: As you know, the literacy is very important to MOYA and connected to the language justice work that we're doing generally.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But that's what I have to share right now.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: No, I think we missed it.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It was right before this one.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But I think what's important to note here is obviously there is an RFP that has challenges that came out in 24.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We're going to continue to ask critical questions around re-looking at that RFP for...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: What we knew were the consequences were going to be and in fact played out, that we continue to invest in adult literacy services.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So even with the expansion, it's still woefully underfunded and a critical service.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I think we see that field is also under attack by the federal government.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So what I would really like to see is like some proactive planning.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Around understanding some of that money being swept away and our city being prepared to step in that gap.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Avilés asks about MOIA's preparation for the 2030 census. Ali explains that ensuring a full count of immigrant New Yorkers requires long-term sustained groundwork, and that MOIA's language access and community partnership investments build essential infrastructure for census outreach.
chapters.title: Councilmember Avilés questions MOIA 2030 census preparation

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, really quick.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: question about the 2030 census, because as we know, it's going to come up a lot faster than we realize.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And, you know, in gearing up for that, knowing how important this is, just wanted to know what MOIA has done in the past few years to prepare for the upcoming census, and then how

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Are you planning to collaborate with the new Office of Census once it's established and sort of what the role of MOIA will play?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, MOIA is really clear.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Unfortunately, the planning for the next, you know, for the previous cycle predates my time and most of our teams, but it's really clear to us that ensuring a full and accurate count of immigrant New Yorkers

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: requires long-term sustained groundwork and not just outreach in the year itself of the census.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So over the past several years, you know, MOIA has worked around, you know, expanding language access and strengthening community partnerships,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: investing in multilingual public education and ethnic media engagement that we believe has helped build the infrastructure that is essential for reaching historically undercounted communities.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Those same systems are critical for the census outreach as well.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And looking forward, you know, we are looking forward to playing an active role in supporting the census efforts by working closely with our community-based organizations and sister agencies.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, sure.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Encarnación asks about family separation preparation resources and language availability. Officials note the flyer is available in 8 languages with Urdu translation underway, plus standby guardianship clinics, KYR trainings in schools, and the family preparedness phone bank.
chapters.title: Chair Encarnación questions family preparedness resources and language expansion

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, we're going to do some rapid questions and responses.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We have until 4 o'clock.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Then we lose our translators and we can't have that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So for family preparedness and family separation, during our roundtables, some of our CBOs shared that they are supporting community members preparing for their family separation, preparing families for possible separation.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I know that MOIA has a flyer posted online.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It is currently available, I think, online in eight languages.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Is there...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Are there plans to expand the amount of languages that it is available for in?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes, so right now I think it's also available in Russian, and we are working on the Urdu translation right now, so it could be available in its top ten.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We're always open to feedback as well if there are additional languages that you might be encountering in your neighborhood.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And if I may share on the RLC, because I just got that other number, just I wanted to make the note that the data that I provided was

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: until the end of March, So there's more work that happened on the DOE side as well as with the rapid response to your collaborative One of the main contract there have been about 137 screenings.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I will just note that although those numbers seem low there are those are very comprehensive complex cases that require

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: you know, very dedicated and experienced attorneys.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: She just wanted to say that because they're very traumatic cases too, that although they seem low, they're actually pretty significant in retrospect.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But thank you for letting me add that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: No, I appreciate that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I want, and it was really about

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: making sure and understanding that people knew about these services and knew how to get connected and that they were availing themselves when they needed support.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so other than the flyer and things like that, what other resources can CBOs look at MOIA to for family separation?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I can take this, Commissioner.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So part of the outreach campaign, which we do do with our outreach team, that's one bucket.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The Commissioner mentioned this at the top, but we also created, we had a MOYA legal hotline bank with ONA.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Back in April, I think April 21st, when we received about 227 calls, we understand that this is an issue that the community really cares about, and that was sort of the way that we provided that outreach.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We worked with ethnic media at the time to spread the word and awareness, so that's another bucket.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We also have the rapid response in schools in which...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Families are provided KYR on what standby guardianship and their options, as well as our legal support centers provided.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: By 26, at least 14 guardianship clinics, observing a total of 364 people.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And then the last one that I'll mention is that our legal technical mentor, the Immigrant Justice Corp, also delivered a five-part training series on special immigration juvenile status,

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: although not directly on family separation, but it's also adjacent to the issue.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I wanted to uplift that work as well.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And do you coordinate with ACS at all for that?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yes, we do.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We talk to them frequently.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I know that there are a lot of changing issues that happen.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So as the commissioner mentioned at the top, we have...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Through our interagency efforts, we do connect with them as well on any federal changes and things to that extent.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay.

chapters.summary: Encarnación asks how MOIA measures compliance with Local Law 30 across agencies, noting a 2023 evaluation found over half of centers in violation. Ali describes oversight, technical assistance, the April convening, and a memo with Deputy Mayor Sue embedding language access as core government function.
chapters.title: Chair Encarnación questions language access compliance across city agencies

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I want to get into language access.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: As you know, we have some of the strongest language access laws in the country.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: What we're seeing, you know, obviously it requires agencies to appoint language access coordinators and to translate.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But in 2023, there was an evaluation by the city's language access, like the secret shopper program.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And it said that more than half of the centers evaluated were in violation of local law 30.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And, you know, since then, the council has been in fiscal 23 through 26.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: The council began investing in the creation of the language service worker cooperatives, as well as the community interpreter bank.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So can you talk to me a little bit about how can MOIA increase collaboration and coordination with the council-funded initiatives, the bank and the language service worker cooperatives?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We've had conversations with the council-funded, both the community interpreter bank organizations and the community language workers.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: On the community interpreter bank specifically, MOIA organized a virtual convening this February where the NYIC has shared information about the services that are being offered by the community interpreter bank.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: 33 different agencies were in fact present.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And our engagement really doesn't end there.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You know, MOIA has also included information about their third-party language resources, including information about the Language Justice Collaborative and other nonprofits in our monthly language access newsletter and agency language access partners.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And how do you measure compliance across city agencies when it comes to those kind of gaps that you see?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You know, MOIA is committed to advancing language justice really as a core principle of how we do our work.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Language access is essential to ensuring that immigrant New Yorkers can fully participate in civic life and accessing critical services and engaging with agencies.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You know, the city's responsibility goes, as I said earlier, beyond compliance.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And this is really...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: for us about building systems that are responsive and inclusive.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You know, MOIA coordinates across the city agencies through a combination of oversight and technical assistance that we offer, and specifically with other agencies.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You know, we monitor this technical assistance to all of the local law authority agencies and convene them on a regular basis.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: As I mentioned in my testimony just this April, we did do our large convening of language access coordinators across agencies to not just underscore the importance of language access, but make sure that there's an opportunity for agencies to

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: share some of their best practices and talk a little bit about...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And that there's a law on it, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Correct.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Compliance is important, I think, in that sense, because the reasoning behind putting that law forward was that we need to start closing those gaps between those agencies.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So, you know, I...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Just pinpoint that because I think that it is, and you know this, and I think that that's where you're getting at with

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: making sure that there's a good relationship and collaboration there, but in terms of making sure that there's compliance, I wanna just, just to understand how

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: we measure that and we fill those gaps and hold them accountable to making sure that they are providing those services.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think part of it is also like having an ongoing, you know, communication.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: with these agencies.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You know, after that convening, DM Sue, Deputy Mayor Julie Sue and I actually issued a memo to all the local law authority agencies, just reiterating

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: not just compliance, I think that we need to have a larger conversation about how do we achieve language justice.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And so we talked in that, you know, memo about how we're embedding language access really as a core government function.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So to your point, it is absolutely the law, but we can go deeper than the spirit of the law if we really want to ensure equitable access for immigrant New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Agreed.

chapters.summary: Encarnación asks about protections for workers who live in NYC but work outside the city. Officials describe meeting with transnational worker advocates and the chief climate officer about heat and climate impacts on workers, showing innovative cross-jurisdiction coordination.
chapters.title: Chair Encarnación questions immigrant worker protections across jurisdictions

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And I know with the immigrant worker protections, because I know you just mentioned Deputy Mayor, sorry, Sue.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There were a lot of discussions, especially in some of our roundtables, around having concerns about workers who may live in the city but work outside of the city and having those protections applied to them.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So does the administration plan to connect with local jurisdictions to talk about how do we coordinate, further protect, and how do we do that knowing that they're not necessarily a sanctuary city like ours?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I mean, these are real concerns that affect people's willingness to move safely for employment and opportunity.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We are open to coordinating with workers across the country.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Do you want to share about the recent roundtable?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, we are.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Taking this international, we actually met with the transnational worker advocates with Deputy Mayor Su, where advocates came from actually Latin America to talk about some of the issues that they have been having.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Another touch point we've had most recently has been with workers and in connection with the chief climate officer to talk about the impacts of heat and climate on workers as well.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So there have been new touch points that we've made with the worker-specific issues that you've lined.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I love that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you so much for that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I think that that's great.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And, you know, it's that kind of innovation that we hope for when we're looking at how do we really support people in a real-world way.

chapters.summary: Encarnación asks about legal services funding for anti-harassment outreach with HPD and collaboration with the new Office of LGBTQIA Affairs. Ali describes meetings with Director Taylor Brown on implementing Local Law 73, assessing TGNC immigrant services, and developing a phased implementation plan.
chapters.title: Chair Encarnación questions anti-harassment outreach with HPD and LGBTQ+ immigrant support

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And as you know, we held a joint hearing with the housing committee to talk about some of the housing issues that we were seeing in the intersectionality around immigration and housing.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Do you know if any of the increased funding for expansion of legal services and executive plan will be involved in anti-harassment outreach in collaboration with HPD?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We don't have that information, but following that hearing, we have been working with them more on some of the gaps that you're hearing laid out and further strengthening the coordination that we have on the ground.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So thank you for hosting that because it was a very timely hearing.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, I really want to make sure that we are...

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Keeping people housed.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I mean, it's one of the only ways that we can really make sure that, and properly housed, right?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Trying to make sure I get through.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Okay, I have two more things really quickly.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: One, I really want to talk about supporting our LGBTQ plus immigrants.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You know, this is a subset of a subset that we cannot forget about and we have to keep uplifting some of the questions there.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So can you please provide an update on your collaboration with Director Taylor Brown of the new Office of LGBTQIA Affairs?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Specifically as it relates to how they meet the needs of our immigrant New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, I think you said it best that it is government's responsibility to support the most vulnerable, and sometimes we have communities that are far more vulnerable than that, and so I appreciate the question.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: You know, MOIA has been meeting regularly with Director Taylor Brown of the Mayor's Office of LGBTQIA Plus Affairs to discuss next steps in implementing specifically Local Law 73, which was passed by the council last year.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: In conjunction with the Mayor's Office of Equality and the Mayor's Office of LGBTQIA Affairs, we are, through that local law, there is a conducting a comprehensive assessment of the services that are currently available to

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: TGNC communities, specifically transgender and gender nonconforming, non-binary.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Intersex immigrants and the findings of this assessment will directly inform a phased implementation plan really designed to address and identify any of the service gaps across the city.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And just in the same vein as the last question, you know, I'm hoping that some of that translates into actual dollars there to support those programs, outreach, and things like that.

chapters.summary: Encarnación asks about MOIA's partnership with the new $2.8M Mayor's Office of Mass Engagement. Ali describes close communication, sharing information about immigrants, and partnering on outreach efforts, noting they're in the same building.
chapters.title: Chair Encarnación questions MOIA partnership with Mayor's Office of Mass Engagement

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So I know that you may not know similarly to the last one if it's there yet, but if we can continue advocacy for that.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: And then lastly, I know the Mayor's Office of Mass Engagement was newly created this year.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It received $2.8 million in baseline funding, started in fiscal 2027.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Can you talk a little bit about your partnership with them, how you may use that office to help support some of the programming that you have?

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Yeah, I'm happy to say that I just spoke to them again yesterday.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So we are very much in close communication and in lockstep in regards to us sharing information regarding immigrants, as well as supporting them with

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: some forward-facing information, coordination, and some outreach efforts that we've made.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I know that we've partnered with them on a couple of things already, and that partnership will continue.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We're also in the same building, so we see each other on the elevators as well, but we are working very closely with them and across teams, not just one, but the whole office as a whole.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I want to be in that elevator.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Come by anytime.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'm going to talk and sprinkle some ideas.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'm going to stake out the elevators now.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: We will be in communication.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There's a lot more.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I had 11 pages of questions, obviously.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: So, you know, I know.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: But, you know, it is extremely complex, as you know.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: There's many layers to it, many subdivisions and answers that I really wanted to get in terms of the executive budget.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: It's difficult to go through and make sure that we properly are supporting each and every aspect of it.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'm going to continue to fight for as much as I can on my end, and I hope that internally you're fighting for our immigrant neighbors.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I know you are, and I'm actually really, really impressed with some of the stuff that I've seen so far and some of the answers to the questions.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: Thank you for being so prepared.

utterances.speaker: UNKNOWN
utterances.text: I'm going to give it over now to my co-chair.
